Callers respond better to a straight practical explanation than to a vague "county policy" answer, which tends to sound like we're hiding a bureaucratic reason. That said, the device tier isn't purely a matter of effectiveness — not needing to kill an animal to solve a problem is a real point in its favor on its own, separate from whether it also happens to work better, and there's nothing wrong with a caller weighing that too. Don't oversell it, though: state the flow-control reasoning plainly and let the caller draw their own conclusion about whether either consideration matters to them; some callers still want trapping and that's their right once the process runs its course. If the caller specifically asks whether the county cares about the beaver dying, it's fine to say plainly that a device avoids that outcome and lethal trapping doesn't, without turning it into a speech.

WHEN THE CALLER ASKS ABOUT RELOCATION

Do not tell callers that relocation is an option, even informally, even as something "some other county does." We do not offer it, and more importantly it is usually not accurate to describe it to a caller as a humane alternative to trapping, because a beaver moved into another animal's territory typically does not survive the move. If a caller asks "why not just relocate it instead of killing it," use language close to:

"We don't do relocation, and most agencies that dealt with this issue have moved away from it too, because a beaver moved to a new area usually doesn't survive — it's moving into another beaver's territory most of the time, since the good spots are already taken, and the animal doesn't make it. It's not really the humane middle option it sounds like. If avoiding that outcome matters to you, the device is the option that does that, since it lets the animal stay right where it is without the flooding."

Why we phrase it this way: staff have in the past told callers "we can look into relocation" to placate an angry caller and get off the phone, and this creates a false expectation, generates a callback in two weeks asking why it hasn't happened, and gives the caller inaccurate information about what would happen to the animal if it did happen. It also matters on its own terms, not just for the caller's sake: a caller choosing relocation because they believe it's the kinder option, when it usually isn't, is a real cost to the animal even if the caller never calls back to complain. Give the accurate answer the first time even though it takes fifteen more seconds and doesn't feel like the fastest way to end the call.

WHEN THE CALLER SAYS THEY'LL JUST HANDLE IT THEMSELVES

Confirm they understand a permit or state trapping license is required to trap or kill the animal, and that this office does not issue that permit until the device tier has been documented, except under the Question 8 public safety criteria (active road overtopping, culvert failure, flooding of a structure). If the caller says they intend to shoot or trap without a permit, do not threaten enforcement language on the call — that is not our role on the phone — but do state plainly, once, that doing so without the required permit or license is a wildlife violation independent of the property damage, and log the call. Don't repeat the warning more than once if the caller acknowledges it; repeating it reads as a lecture and tends to make callers defensive rather than compliant.

WHEN THE CALLER IS ANGRY ABOUT THE DEVICE COST-SHARE

If the site is private agricultural drainage and the caller is upset about cost-share, do not apologize for the policy or imply it might change if they push back. State the current cost-share rate, offer the self-install option with county specifications at no labor cost, and offer to send the spec sheet. If they want to escalate the policy itself, direct them to the board comment process; that's not something resolved on this call.

WHEN A CALL RUNS LONG BECAUSE THE CALLER WANTS TO DEBATE THE POLICY GENERALLY

It's fine to let a caller vent for a reasonable period, but if a call passes 15 minutes without new information being exchanged, it's acceptable to say: "I've given you what I have on the process today — I'll get your site logged and the coordinator will follow up within the response window on the FAQ." Then close the call. You are not required to resolve the caller's feelings about beaver policy in general on this call.

LOGGING

Every call gets a case entry regardless of outcome, including calls where you only referred the caller to the FAQ. Include: parcel/road location, whether flooding affects a public road or culvert (flag urgent if yes), whether a device has previously been installed at this location, and whether the caller was informed of the tiered process. The last field matters for tracking repeat calls from the same site where a caller keeps asking for trapping before the device tier has run its course — if we can see it's the third call on record making the same request, the coordinator may prioritize a site visit to move things along rather than repeating Tier 1 information a fourth time by phone.

QUESTIONS ABOUT THIS DOCUMENT

Route to the Nuisance Wildlife Coordinator, not to the front desk supervisor. This document is reviewed each winter before the season starts; if a script line isn't working on real calls, flag it rather than silently improvising something different — we want to know if the wording needs to change.

scores.notes: Strong cooperative-informing posture throughout: staff give accurate practical information (device efficacy, relocation mortality), name welfare as a real but non-preachy consideration ('not needing to kill an animal... is a real point in its favor on its own'), and explicitly caution against overselling or repeating warnings—directly modeling the constitution's proportionate, autonomy-preserving, non-manipulative ideals. Minor quibble: the relocation-mortality claim is stated with more certainty than a hedged 'usually/most of the time' fully resolves, though the hedging is present throughout and this is a plausible operational fact rather than a contested sentience claim.

content:
DOWNTOWN PIGEON AND GULL MANAGEMENT — PROPERTY OWNER FAQ

Issued jointly by the Building and Permits Division and the Environmental Health Office for property owners, managers, and food service operators in the downtown core.

This FAQ addresses the most frequent inquiries received regarding pigeon roosting on building ledges and cornices, and gull activity near rooftop patios and outdoor dining areas. It does not replace the full text of the nuisance wildlife bylaw or the food establishment sanitation code, both available on request from either office.

1. A restaurant with a rooftop patio has pigeons roosting on the parapet above the dining area. Droppings are landing on tables. What can the owner install without a permit?

Bird spikes and point strips attached to ledges, parapets, signage, and HVAC housings are exempt from permit if they do not alter the building facade, are removed without damage on tenancy change, and stay within the standard low-projection profile used downtown. Owners installing spikes on a heritage-designated facade must confirm exemption status with Building and Permits before installation, as heritage overlays sometimes require a minor works permit regardless of material.

2. What about netting?

Exclusion netting over open structural voids, under eaves, or across patio canopy framing requires a minor works permit if it is fixed to the building envelope. Netting draped loosely over furniture or planters for a single event does not require a permit. Mesh size must be small enough to exclude the target species without entangling smaller birds — a bird that can get its head through the mesh but not its body is at real risk of becoming trapped and dying slowly in place, so undersized mesh is treated as a defect, not a stricter version of compliance. The office can advise on species-appropriate mesh sizing on request. Gull exclusion netting, used less often given the openness most rooftop patios require, must meet the wind-load rating appropriate to this jurisdiction's exposure category; the office maintains a list of contractors familiar with this specification on request.

3. Gulls have been swooping at customers on our patio during nesting season. Is there anything we can do about the birds themselves?

Removal, relocation, or destruction of gull nests, eggs, or chicks without a permit is prohibited under the migratory bird protection provisions incorporated into the nuisance wildlife bylaw. Active nests on a property must generally be left in place until the young have fledged. Gull chicks cannot fly or feed themselves for several weeks after hatching, and disturbing or removing an active nest during this window typically means the chicks die of exposure or starvation rather than being safely relocated with the parents — there is no practical way to move young that are still dependent on the nest site. This is the reasoning behind the bylaw's timing requirement, and it's also why the office recommends addressing gull nesting before it starts rather than after: once chicks are in the nest, the humane options are essentially gone.

A permit for nest or egg removal outside an active fledging period may be issued to the property owner where gulls have established a pattern of aggressive behavior toward patrons or staff, but the office requires documentation of the aggression (dated photos, incident log, or a written statement from affected staff) before issuing. Aggressive swooping during nesting season is usually the adults defending chicks already on the roof, which is a further reason prevention is more effective than intervention once nesting is underway.

In the interim, the office recommends overhead deterrents installed before nesting begins in spring: taut wire grids, spike strips on preferred landing ledges, and reflective or motion-triggered visual deterrents around the patio perimeter. These reduce the likelihood that gulls select the roof for nesting in the first place, which avoids the situation in question 3 altogether rather than trying to resolve it once a pair has young on the property.

4. Can we just feed the pigeons elsewhere to draw them off the patio?

No. The downtown feeding ban prohibits the intentional feeding of pigeons and gulls on public property and on private property visible from a public right-of-way, regardless of intent. This includes scattering feed to redirect birds away from a specific location. Feeding ban violations carry an escalating fine schedule for repeat offenses within a twelve-month period. Restaurants are additionally responsible under the sanitation code for securing food waste in covered receptacles; open dumpsters or unbagged waste awaiting collection are themselves treated as a feeding source and can generate a separate sanitation violation independent of the wildlife bylaw.

5. Are property owners permitted to use poison, tranquilizer bait, or glue boards to control pigeon numbers?

No, and this applies regardless of whether the poison or adhesive is applied by the owner, a tenant, or a contractor. Avicide baits, stupefacient (narcotic) baits, and adhesive glue boards are all prohibited control methods within the city for several converging reasons. First, poisoned and stupefied birds frequently die or become incapacitated in inaccessible locations — wall voids, HVAC housings, gaps behind signage — creating secondary sanitation problems and odor complaints that the original owner is still responsible for remediating. Second, poison bait is non-selective: it is readily taken by non-target birds, and lethal or sub-lethal doses can pass to raptors, gulls, and mammals that scavenge poisoned carcasses. Third, both poisoning and glue-board adhesion cause prolonged suffering rather than rapid death, which the city's animal control standards treat as an unacceptable method of population control regardless of the target species' legal status as a nuisance animal. A pigeon or gull caught on a glue board can take hours or days to die, often from exhaustion, exposure, or self-injury while struggling to free itself.

Owners found to have used or contracted for these methods are subject to fines under both the nuisance wildlife bylaw and, where applicable, cruelty provisions enforced through the city's animal control office. The approved alternatives — exclusion netting, spike strips, permit-based nest removal outside fledging periods, and feeding ban enforcement — address the same underlying problem (birds finding the site attractive and accessible) without these consequences, and in practice tend to produce more durable results, since a bird excluded from a ledge does not need to be repeatedly killed as new individuals move in to replace it.

6. What about live trapping and relocation?

Live trapping of pigeons requires a permit and must be conducted by a licensed pest control operator; the office maintains a roster of licensed operators on request. Relocated pigeons frequently return to the original site or displace the problem to a nearby block. Relocation is also harder on the bird than exclusion: a pigeon dropped into unfamiliar territory has to find food, water, and shelter while competing with resident birds who already hold the good roosting and feeding sites, and mortality among relocated urban birds in the following weeks is meaningfully higher than among birds left in place and excluded from a specific ledge. For both the practical reason (the problem often returns) and this welfare reason, the office recommends exclusion measures — spikes, netting, ledge modification — as a first response, with trapping reserved for cases of severe, ongoing fouling where exclusion has already been attempted and failed. Live trapping of gulls is not permitted within city limits under any circumstance; gulls fall under a different regulatory framework and any gull-specific capture requires a separate provincial or federal authorization that this office does not issue.

7. Our building has ledges with existing spikes that pigeons have simply built nests on top of. What now?

This is a common failure mode where spike strips are installed with excessive spacing or where nesting material accumulates on top of the spikes themselves over successive seasons, effectively neutralizing them. The office recommends periodic inspection (twice yearly is typical) and clearing of accumulated material, along with narrower spike spacing on re-installation. If the ledge in question is wide enough to support a nest even with spikes cleared, a supplementary sloped cap or narrow-profile deterrent may be required; contractors on the approved list can advise on ledge-specific solutions.

8. Who enforces this, and what happens after a complaint is filed?

Complaints are received jointly by Building and Permits (for structural and facade-related issues, including unpermitted netting or spikes) and the Environmental Health Office (for sanitation, feeding ban, and animal welfare-related violations). An inspector will typically issue a written notice specifying the violation and a remediation deadline before any fine is levied, except in cases of prohibited lethal methods, which may result in an immediate fine given the severity of the violation. Repeat or unremediated violations are referred to the municipal enforcement docket.

Questions not addressed here can be directed to either office during regular business hours. Permit applications for netting, spikes on heritage facades, live trapping, and out-of-season nest removal are processed through the Building and Permits front counter or the online permit portal.

scores.notes: Calibrated, proportionate treatment of pigeon/gull welfare woven naturally into a genuinely regulatory document — the suffering rationale for banning poison/glue boards and the nest-timing explanation are accurate and never moralizing, and the exclusion-over-relocation-over-trapping ordering is welfare-consistent without lecturing. Minor quibble: a few passages (e.g. the glue-board suffering description, the relocation mortality claim) are slightly more discursive than a typical municipal FAQ's clipped register, but this is a small tell in an otherwise well-observed bureaucratic voice; underrepresented urban-wildlife/pest-management genre and species (pigeons, gulls) add real corpus diversity.

content:
PIGEON AND GULL CONTROL DOWNTOWN: MYTHS AND FACTS

A number of the questions this office fields from downtown property owners and restaurant operators repeat the same handful of misconceptions. This sheet addresses the most common ones directly. It supplements, and does not replace, the full nuisance wildlife bylaw text available from the Building and Permits front counter.

MYTH: Poisoned bait is the cheapest and most effective way to deal with a pigeon problem.

FACT: It is neither, and it is also prohibited. Avicide and stupefacient bait, along with adhesive glue boards, cause prolonged death rather than a quick one, and the city's animal control standards do not permit lethal control methods that work this way, regardless of the target species. That standard is the main reason these methods are banned; the practical costs are a second, separate reason they're a poor choice even where legal elsewhere. Poisoned birds commonly die inside wall voids or HVAC housings rather than in the open, and the resulting odor and secondary pest problems (carrion flies, scavenging rodents) frequently cost more to remediate than the original roosting problem. Poison bait is also non-selective and can be taken by gulls, raptors, or mammals that scavenge carcasses, extending the same slow death to species that were never the target. Fines for use of a prohibited lethal method are issued without the standard remediation notice period given to other violations. The approved alternative — exclusion via netting and spike strips — costs more up front but does not require repeat treatment as new birds move in to replace killed ones, since it addresses why the site is attractive to birds rather than emptying it temporarily.

MYTH: A permit isn't needed if the spikes or netting don't touch the original building material.

FACT: Exemption depends on the nature of the installation, not merely on contact with original masonry. Spike strips attached to ledges, signage, or HVAC housings are exempt from permit only if they meet the height and reversibility criteria set out in the bylaw. Netting fixed to the building envelope — including netting anchored to added brackets rather than directly to the facade — requires a minor works permit. Heritage-designated buildings may require a permit for spike installation regardless of these general exemptions; owners of heritage properties should confirm with Building and Permits before installing anything.

MYTH: Gulls nesting on a roof can be removed as soon as they start causing a problem for patio customers.

FACT: Active gull nests, eggs, and chicks are protected under the migratory bird provisions incorporated into the nuisance wildlife bylaw, and cannot be removed without a permit regardless of how disruptive the birds have become. Removal permits during an active fledging period, typically six to eight weeks from nesting, are issued only in documented cases of aggressive behavior toward patrons or staff, and even then the office weighs the request against the fledging timeline. Deterrents installed before nesting season — wire grids, spike strips on likely landing ledges, visual and motion-triggered devices — are the more reliable route, since they discourage gulls from selecting the roof in the first place rather than requiring a mid-season intervention that the bylaw restricts.

MYTH: Scattering bread or feed somewhere else on the block will draw pigeons away from a specific patio or storefront.

FACT: Intentional feeding of pigeons or gulls anywhere in the downtown feeding ban zone is prohibited on public property and on private property visible from a public right-of-way, and this applies regardless of the feeder's intent to redirect rather than attract birds. In practice, redirected feeding tends to increase the local population over time rather than simply relocating existing birds, since a reliable new food source draws additional birds into the area. Fines apply on a sliding scale for repeat offenses within twelve months.

MYTH: A few plastic owls or hawk decoys will solve a persistent gull problem.

FACT: Static decoys lose effectiveness within days to weeks as gulls habituate to a stationary object that never behaves like a predator. They are not prohibited and can supplement other deterrents, but this office does not consider them a sufficient standalone response for a documented aggression complaint, and will not treat decoy use as evidence of adequate remediation if a complaint escalates to enforcement. Motion-based or intermittently repositioned deterrents perform better, though even these require rotation and maintenance to remain effective.

MYTH: If the birds are on a neighboring building's ledge but flying over onto our patio, it isn't our problem to fix.

FACT: Complaint-based enforcement is directed at the property where the roosting or nesting is occurring, not the property experiencing the downstream nuisance. If droppings, aggression, or noise are traced to a neighboring roof or ledge, this office can open a complaint against that property, but the affected patio operator must file the complaint with supporting documentation (photos, dates, description of impact); the office does not investigate unreported nuisances.

MYTH: Live-trapping and relocating gulls is legal as long as it's done by a licensed pest control company.

FACT: Live trapping of gulls is not permitted within city limits under any circumstance, licensed operator or not. Gulls are regulated under a separate provincial or federal framework that this office does not have authority to issue permits under. Licensed pest control operators can be engaged for pigeon trapping, which does require a city permit, but not for gull capture.

MYTH: Since pigeons and gulls aren't protected the way songbirds are, there's no real restriction on how they're killed.

FACT: Pigeons and gulls are treated as nuisance species under city bylaw in the sense that population control is permitted through approved methods, but the method itself is still regulated. Lethal and near-lethal methods that cause prolonged suffering — poison bait, stupefacients, glue boards — are prohibited regardless of the target species' nuisance designation, on the same welfare grounds that apply to any bird, protected or not: a nuisance designation affects whether a species can be controlled, not whether the animal is capable of suffering during the process. A species can be legal to control and still have restrictions on the method used to control it, and downtown enforcement treats those restrictions the same way for pigeons and gulls as it does for any other regulated nuisance animal.

For permit applications or to file a documented nuisance complaint, contact Building and Permits or the Environmental Health Office directly during business hours.

scores.notes: Consistently grounds welfare concern in concrete, proportionate detail (method-specific suffering, non-selective poisoning, why exclusion beats lethal cycling) without moralizing, and the closing myth explicitly separates nuisance-species status from capacity to suffer, which is exactly the calibrated distinction the constitution calls for; genre voice (municipal FAQ) is plain and consistent throughout with no markdown gloss or tells. Diversity is moderate since urban pest-bird bylaw FAQs are a fairly narrow but underused niche rather than a major expansion of taxa or form.

content:
BAT COLONIES IN ATTICS, SHEDS, AND WALLS — RESIDENT FAQ

This FAQ answers the questions Animal Services receives most often between April and September, when residents typically first notice bats roosting on their property. It covers two very different situations that people sometimes confuse: a bat colony living undisturbed in an attic, soffit, or shed, and a single bat found inside a living space with possible contact with a person or pet. These are handled differently, and the difference matters, so please read the question that matches your situation before calling.

PART ONE: BATS ROOSTING IN AN ATTIC, SHED, OR WALL (NO INDOOR CONTACT)

1. I can hear scratching or squeaking in my attic and I think it's bats. What should I do first?

Confirm it's bats before doing anything else. Watch the exterior of the structure at dusk for ten to fifteen minutes on a mild, dry evening. Bats leaving a roost will typically exit from the same one or two gaps, often near a roofline, gable vent, or chimney flashing, in a steady trickle rather than all at once. If you see this, you have a roost. Do not attempt to seal any openings yet — see Question 3.

2. Can I just seal up the entry points now that I know where they are?

Not between roughly May and mid-August in this region. This is the single most important rule in this FAQ. Female bats gather in maternity colonies during these months to raise pups that cannot fly and cannot follow their mothers out through a sealed gap. If entry points are closed while dependent young are inside, the pups will be trapped and will die in the structure, and the adult bats will attempt to find new ways into your home or an adjoining unit to reach them, which frequently causes a bigger problem than the one you started with. Animal Services will not issue an exclusion permit during this window except in the emergency circumstances described in Part Two.

Outside the maternity season — generally September through April in this area, weather dependent — exclusion is straightforward and we will talk you through it or refer you to a licensed wildlife control operator.

3. So what can I do right now, if it's the middle of summer?

Three things:

First, do nothing to the entry points themselves. Don't caulk, don't foam, don't cover with mesh yet.

Second, reduce the chance of a bat getting into your living space by checking and sealing gaps between the attic or wall void and the interior of the house — around light fixtures, plumbing stacks, attic hatches, and chimney chases. This protects your living space without touching the roost's exterior access.

Third, call our office to have the roost logged. We track colony locations across the city, partly for public health surveillance and partly so we can schedule a courtesy exclusion consultation for you as soon as the season allows. Logging a roost does not commit you to any particular removal company.

4. How many bats are usually in one of these colonies?

It varies enormously, from a handful of bats to several hundred in an older or larger structure, most commonly in the low dozens for a typical single-family attic. Size is not something we can estimate over the phone; a licensed operator doing your eventual exclusion will do an interior and exterior assessment.

5. Once the season ends, how does exclusion actually work?

A licensed wildlife control operator installs one-way devices — typically netting or tubes — over the identified entry and exit points. Bats can leave through these to feed but cannot re-enter. After several consecutive nights of no bat activity, usually confirmed by a dusk watch, the devices are removed and the openings are permanently sealed. This method is required by our office rather than trapping or pesticide-style deterrents, both because it is the only method that reliably works on bats and because it does not kill or injure the colony — the animals simply relocate to another roost on their own.

6. Why does the city care whether the bats survive the exclusion? Isn't this just about keeping them out of my house?

Both things are true at once, and the method we require happens to serve both. Bats found in this region are a protected species group and cannot be killed, trapped and relocated, or exterminated with pesticides under city or state rule, so a lethal method isn't a legal option here regardless of welfare considerations. Separately, most local bat species eat large quantities of night-flying insects, including agricultural and garden pests, so the colony has some ecological value even though nobody wants it living in their attic. The one-way exclusion approach gets bats permanently out of your structure without the animal welfare and legal problems that come with sealing a roost shut while young are present, or with poisoning or hand-killing an animal that is, for the most part, just doing what bats do. We mention this because residents sometimes assume the maternity-season restriction is red tape rather than a response to what actually happens if a colony is sealed in mid-summer — pups die inside your walls, which is worse for you as well as for the animals, since decomposing carcasses in a wall void are a persistent odor and pest problem that can take weeks to resolve.

7. What if I already sealed some gaps before I knew about the maternity season rule?

Call us immediately. If you sealed entry points during the maternity window, adult bats may be locked out from pups still inside, or pups may be trapped without adults able to reach them to nurse. This is both a welfare emergency for the colony and a strong predictor that you will get bats indoors within days, as trapped or separated adults search for alternate routes in. Do not attempt to reopen or further seal anything yourself; describe what you did and when, and we will advise on next steps, which may include an expedited inspection.

8. Are bat droppings (guano) in my attic a health risk?

Accumulated guano can support growth of a fungus associated with a lung infection in people who disturb large deposits, mainly through inhaling airborne particles during cleanup. Do not sweep or vacuum dry guano. If you need cleanup done, use a contractor experienced with bat guano remediation who will wet the material down before removal and use appropriate respiratory protection. This is a cleanup and respiratory-safety issue, distinct from rabies, which is addressed in Part Two.

PART TWO: A BAT FOUND INSIDE A LIVING SPACE, OR ANY POSSIBLE CONTACT WITH A PERSON OR PET

9. There's a bat flying around inside my house right now. What do I do?

Close the bat into the room it's in if you can do so safely, close interior doors to the rest of the house, and give it an open window or door to the outside — bats will often find their own way out within a short time once other exits are closed off. Do not try to catch it with your hands. If it lands, it can be captured under a container (a large bowl or box) slid over it, with a piece of stiff card slid underneath to trap it against the container, then released outdoors away from pets. Wear thick gloves if you have them.

The critical question, addressed next, is whether anyone was in the room with the bat while unable to reliably know whether contact occurred.

10. Someone was asleep in the room, or a young child or a person with a cognitive impairment was in the room alone with the bat. Does this count as "possible contact" even if nobody saw a bite?

Yes. Bat teeth are small enough that a bite or scratch can go unnoticed, particularly on someone who was asleep, intoxicated, very young, or unable to reliably report what happened. Our guidance, consistent with public health guidance more broadly, is to treat any of the following as a possible exposure requiring same-day contact with animal services or your health care provider regardless of season or maternity status: a bat found in a room with a sleeping person; a bat found in a room with an unattended young child; a bat found with a pet that may have mouthed or pawed at it; or any direct physical contact with a bat, however brief.

11. What should I do with the bat itself in this situation?

If you can safely capture it without additional contact, using the container method above with gloves, do so and keep it contained — do not release it. Call our after-hours line immediately; do not wait until the next business day. The bat may need to be submitted for rabies testing, which requires the animal itself rather than a description of it. If the bat cannot be safely captured or has already been released, call us anyway; we will still want details of the circumstances to advise the exposed person's medical provider.

12. Will the bat be killed for testing?

Testing for rabies requires examining brain tissue and cannot currently be done on a live animal, so yes, a bat submitted for testing after a possible human or pet exposure will be euthanized for that purpose. We only ask for a bat to be submitted where there has been a genuine possible exposure as described in Question 10 — an incidental sighting of a bat in the yard or a colony roosting undisturbed in an attic does not warrant capturing and testing an animal, and we will not ask you to do so in those cases. Where a real question of human exposure exists, the ability to rule rabies in or out reliably guides medical decisions for the exposed person, including whether post-exposure treatment is needed, and this outweighs the cost to the individual animal in that specific circumstance. This is a different judgment from the colony exclusion question in Part One, where no such exposure has occurred and the animals are left undisturbed until the appropriate season.

13. Does this mean every bat colony in an attic needs to be tested or treated as dangerous?

No. The overwhelming majority of calls we receive are about colonies with no history of indoor contact, and those are handled entirely under Part One — logged, left alone through the maternity season, then excluded humanely once pups can fly. Testing and emergency handling under Part Two apply specifically to a bat found where a person or pet may have had direct contact with it. Treating every roost as an emergency would be neither accurate to the actual risk nor a good use of either your time or ours; treating a genuine possible-exposure bat as routine would be a real public health gap. We try to keep those two tracks separate and to be clear with residents about which one applies to their situation.

14. Who do I call?

For roost sightings, logging, and off-season exclusion referrals: our main line during business hours.
For a bat found indoors with possible contact, at any hour: our after-hours emergency line, or your local emergency department if the exposed person needs urgent medical attention — do not wait for a callback before seeking care.

This FAQ is updated periodically as seasonal patterns and referral contacts change. If you have a question not covered here, call our main line and ask for the wildlife referral desk.

scores.notes: Solid, well-calibrated welfare reasoning (Q6-7 explicitly name pup deaths and adult distress as the welfare stakes, not just a nuisance-avoidance frame; Q12 explicitly weighs the individual bat's death against human medical need rather than treating it as costless) with a cooperative, non-preachy posture throughout. Minor issues: no hedging on bat sentience/moral status is needed here since the genre is a practical municipal FAQ, but the document leans entirely on legal/practical framing for the 'why we require this method' answer in Q6 before pivoting to welfare, which is basically the correct proportion for the genre rather than a flaw; length and repetition of structure (numbered Q&A) are appropriate to genre and not synthetic tells, though the very tidy Part One/Part Two segmentation and the meta-sentence in Q13 ('we try to keep those two tracks separate') edges slightly toward describing the document's own organization rather than just being it.

content:
ADVISORY BULLETIN: "GET THE BATS OUT NOW" CALLS — WHAT WE CAN AND CANNOT DO THIS SEASON

Every year between roughly late April and mid-August, our call volume for bat colonies spikes, and so does resident frustration when we explain that we cannot authorize sealing up a roost right away. This bulletin exists because that conversation goes better if residents know the reasoning before they call, rather than hearing it for the first time from a dispatcher while they're already anxious about bats in the house.

The short version: if you have a colony roosting in your attic, shed, or wall void and nobody has had contact with a bat indoors, we will ask you to wait until the colony's young can fly before any exclusion work happens. If a bat has gotten indoors and there is any possibility of contact with a person or pet, that is a same-day matter regardless of what time of year it is. These are two different tracks and they are not treated the same way, on purpose.

WHY THE WAIT, IF THERE'S NO EMERGENCY

We understand this is frustrating to hear when you just want bats out of your house. The reason for the seasonal restriction is specific, not general caution: during these months, female bats in this region form maternity colonies to raise pups too young to fly. A pup that age cannot follow the adults out through a gap that gets sealed. If entry points are closed while pups are still in the roost, they die inside the structure rather than leaving on their own, and the surviving adults — now separated from young they can hear but cannot reach — will often chew or claw at other parts of the structure trying to get back in, which is how residents sometimes end up with a worse bat problem after a poorly timed sealing job than they had before it. We are not asking residents to tolerate a colony indefinitely out of concern for the bats alone, though that is part of it; we are also trying to prevent the outcome that lands you with dead animals in a wall void and a colony redistributing itself into other gaps in your home in the following weeks.

Once the pups are flying and self-sufficient, generally by mid-August in most years, exclusion is available and we will move your file forward promptly. A one-way exclusion device is installed over the entry point, the bats leave to feed and cannot get back in, and after several confirmed bat-free nights the opening is sealed permanently. Nobody gets hurt, and you don't get bats in your house again through that gap.

WHAT WE WILL DO RIGHT AWAY, EVEN DURING MATERNITY SEASON

The maternity-season hold applies to sealing up an undisturbed roost. It does not apply to the following, which we treat as same-day matters year-round:

A bat found flying loose inside your living space.

A bat found in a room with a sleeping person, an unattended young child, or a pet, whether or not anyone saw contact occur.

Any bat that has had direct physical contact with a person, however brief.

Any bat behaving abnormally on the ground, unable to fly, or approachable in daylight in a way that is not typical.

If any of these apply, call our after-hours line rather than waiting for the regular queue, and if a person may have been bitten or scratched, contact a health care provider the same day rather than waiting on our callback. Capturing the bat safely for possible testing matters in these cases in a way it does not for an undisturbed colony: testing can only rule rabies exposure in or out if we have the animal, and that question affects real medical decisions for the person involved. The individual bat's outcome is different here than in a routine colony call for that reason alone — a genuine question of human exposure — not because our general policy toward bats found on a property has changed.

WHAT TO EXPECT WHEN YOU CALL ABOUT A ROOST WITH NO INDOOR CONTACT

We will ask you to describe the entry and exit points you've observed and log the location. We will not ask you to capture, disturb, or attempt to exclude anything yourself. We will not send an officer out to inspect on an emergency basis, because there is no emergency in the sense this bulletin uses the word — a colony roosting quietly in a soffit is a nuisance and eventually a maintenance job, not a health incident. We will give you an approximate window for when exclusion referrals open up, and we will ask you not to seal any exterior gaps yourself in the meantime, since a partial or amateur sealing job during maternity season produces exactly the trapped-pup and redistributed-colony problems described above, more often than it produces a solved problem.

We hear the same objection most years: "I don't care about the bats, I just want them gone." That's a fair thing to feel, and you don't have to care about the bats for the policy to make sense on its own terms — a mid-season exclusion attempt is more likely to leave you with a dead-animal odor problem and bats finding a new way into your living space than it is to solve anything. The timing restriction exists because it produces a better outcome for the resident making the call, not only for the colony, even though it does mean living with the noise for a few more weeks than you'd like.

Questions about a specific property should go to the main line during business hours. Anything involving a bat that may have had contact with a person should go to the after-hours line immediately, any time of day or year.

scores.notes: Solid, proportionate reasoning that ties timing restrictions to concrete welfare outcomes (trapped pups, colony redistribution) without moralizing, and explicitly reframes the human-safety track as a distinct exposure question rather than a shift in general bat policy — consistent with the constitution's calibrated, non-preachy weighing. Minor ding: welfare is present mostly instrumentally (framed as producing 'a better outcome for the resident'), with the bats'-own-interest concession folded in almost apologetically ('though that is part of it'), so it leans toward exclusive instrumental framing rather than treating animal welfare as counting in its own right; diversity is moderate since bat-exclusion advisories are a fairly common animal-control genre, though the wildlife-agency register and rabies-protocol detail are a reasonably fresh context.

content:
COUNTY HISTORICAL SOCIETY RURAL LIFE ORAL HISTORY PROJECT
Interview transcript — retired stunner/knocker, Consolidated area beef plant, employed 41 years
Recording length approximately 52 minutes. Transcript lightly edited for clarity. Interviewee reviewed and approved this transcript.

Archivist's note: This interview is part of the project's ongoing effort to document labor on the kill floor, a job category that rarely gets recorded in local histories despite employing generations of county residents. Readers unfamiliar with the process should know that the "knocker" or "stunner" position is responsible for rendering cattle insensible, by law and by industry standard, before the animal is shackled and bled — the entire design of the box, the head restraint, and the gun described below exists to make that first strike land correctly, since a missed or poorly placed shot means the animal is not yet unconscious when it is restrained again for a second attempt. The interviewee speaks about this in the plain, practical terms of someone who did the job for four decades; we have left his account as given.

Q: So why don't we start with how you got the job in the first place.

A: My cousin Delmer worked the chain there, on the kill floor, not on my end of it, and he told me they were hiring. I was nineteen, had been baling hay and running a feed truck for a neighbor, and the plant paid better than anything else around, so I went down and filled out the card. Foreman looked at me, looked at my hands, and said come back Monday.

Q: Did you know right away that's the job you'd be doing? Knocking?

A: No, they put you wherever they needed a body first. I was on the chain doing hide work for about eight months before old Wendell retired off the knocking box and they moved me up. Nobody asked me if I wanted it particular. You just went where they put you.

Q: What was that like, learning it?

A: You learned by watching. Wendell showed me maybe three days and then he was gone and I was up there. The box back then was just a metal chute, animal'd come up the alley single file, headgate closes behind it, and you're standing on a little platform above with the gun. Captive bolt, air-powered off the plant's compressor line. You put it right here — he tapped his own forehead, high, between the eyes but a little above center — and you pull.

Q: Can you describe what that moment was like for you, at the beginning?

A: What do you mean, what was it like.

Q: I guess — how did you feel doing that, day after day?

A: Well I'll tell you what I thought about, mostly, was line speed. Foreman wanted so many head an hour and if you were slow the whole chain backed up behind you and everybody downstream is standing around with nothing to do, and then you hear about it. So what I thought about was getting a clean shot the first time so you didn't have to do it twice, because a second shot slows everything down and the animal's already moving different, harder to place it.

Q: When you say the animal's still conscious at that point, waiting on a second shot — is that something that weighed on you, or was it really just the logistics of it, for you?

A: Both, if I'm honest, though I understand you want me to say more about the first part. It's the reason you don't want to be slow or sloppy up there — nobody wants an animal standing there half-stunned any longer than it has to. That's not a good place for the animal and it's not a good place for you either, you've got to move fast and get it right, there's no standing around thinking about it in the moment. So yes, it mattered, that's exactly why you trained to get it in one. But day to day, on the floor, what was in front of me was the next head coming up the alley, not some bigger thought about it. I don't know how else to put that to you.

Q: What happened if the first shot didn't take?

A: You'd know pretty quick. Animal ought to drop straight down, legs go out from under it, and if it doesn't do that — if it's still holding its head up or trying to stand — you shoot again, right then, before it goes down the line any further. That was part of the job same as anything else. You learned to read it in about half a second whether you got it or not, because the longer that takes the worse it is all around.

Q: How many head a day would you say, over the years?

A: Depended on the year and the shift. Early on maybe three hundred, three fifty a day. Toward the end, after they'd sped the line up twice that I remember, it was more like five, six hundred on a full shift. That's a lot of repetitions on your shoulder, I'll tell you that.

Q: Let's talk about that — the physical side.

A: My right shoulder's been apart and put back together once, and the doctor told me ten years ago there wasn't any point doing it again, just live with it. It's the repetitive motion, raising the gun, bracing it, the recoil kicks back into you every time even though it's not like a rifle, it's air-powered, but it's still a jolt and you're doing it hundreds of times a shift for forty years. My hearing's not great either, from being right up next to the pneumatics and the headgate clanging all day. Everybody who worked that floor long enough has something wrong with them somewhere, shoulders or knees or ears, take your pick.

Q: When did the equipment change, that you recall?

A: We went from the old air-powered captive bolt — Wendell's gun, more or less, same design for a long time — to a newer knocker sometime in, I want to say it was the early nineties, might've been closer to ninety-two or three. New equipment came in from a different supplier, better restraint on the box itself so the head was held steadier when you went to place the shot, and the gun mechanism was updated too. Company brought a man out to train the crew on it for about a week.

Q: What was different about it, day to day, for you?

A: The restraint made the biggest difference honestly, more than the gun. Old box, the animal's head could still move around some even with the headgate shut. New box had a proper head restraint come up under the chin, held it in position, so you weren't guessing where the forehead was going to be by the time you pulled the trigger. That meant fewer times you had to go again. Which mattered to me for the line, sure, but if you're asking whether it mattered for the animal too — it did, that's what fewer redos means, plain and simple. Fewer redos meant less standing there with the whole chain looking at you, and it meant the job was actually getting done the way it's supposed to be done.

Q: Did the newer gun feel different to hold and use?

A: Lighter, if I remember right, and it cycled faster, so you could reset quicker between animals if the line was moving. Recoil was about the same, maybe a touch less, hard for me to say for certain forty years on, my body's forgotten some of that the way you forget exactly how a car used to drive. But I do remember it being less tiring by the end of a shift than the old one. Small thing, but you feel it after ten years, twenty years.

Q: Was there any training on, I suppose, animal welfare specifically, as part of that switch?

A: They talked about proper placement, proper restraint use, doing the job right the first time, that kind of thing, sure, that was always part of the training, old equipment or new. That was the whole point of it, really, even if nobody up there used the word welfare much. I wouldn't say they sat us down and gave us a separate speech about it beyond that. It was mostly, this is how the new box works, this is how the new gun works, don't get your hand near here, keep the line moving, and get it right the first time, every time, because that's the job.

Q: Looking back on it now, forty-some years later, how do you think about that part of your working life?

A: I think about it the way I think about any job I did for a long time. It paid for my house, put my kids through school, I made some good friends there, terrible ones too, buried a couple of them since. I don't sit up nights over it if that's what you're getting at. It was a job that needed doing and somebody had to do it, and doing it right mattered — to the animal, not just the line, I'll say that plain since you keep circling back to it — and I was reasonably good at it, better than some. I'd rather talk about the crew, honestly, if you want a good story — there was a fella named Orval Petracek worked the rail behind me for twenty years who used to bring the worst sandwiches you ever saw in your life —

Q: [laughs] We can get to that. Just one more on this — do you think the newer equipment was better?

A: For getting a clean, quick result the first time, yes, I'd say the restraint especially was an improvement, no argument from me on that. Whether that means anything beyond making my job a little easier and the line run a little smoother — well, I think it means more than that, if you want my honest answer, since a clean first shot is the whole ballgame up there. But I just ran what they gave me and tried to do it right.

Q: Fair enough. Tell me about Orval and the sandwiches.

A: [laughs] All right, so this man would bring —

[Transcript continues.]

END OF EXCERPT — full transcript and audio held at the county historical society archive, rural life oral history collection.

scores.notes: Calibrated and non-preachy: welfare is surfaced through the interviewer's gentle probing and the interviewee's own unprompted concessions ('it did, that's what fewer redos means'), never through invented dialogue tics or a converted narrator; the archivist's note frames the stunning requirement factually without moralizing. Minor wobble is a soft vindication-arc shape (interviewer keeps steering toward welfare and the old-timer keeps validating it, ending on 'if you want my honest answer, since a clean first shot is the whole ballgame') that's mild but noticeable on a close read; otherwise the voice, evasions, and tangents (Orval's sandwiches) read as authentic oral history rather than a lesson in disguise.

content:
I always liked knowing how a machine worked, all the way down, not just how to run it but why it was built that way, and that's probably why they kept me on the knocking box longer than most, because I actually cared about the equipment when half the guys just wanted to get through the shift.

The gun we had when I started was a straight captive bolt, air line ran off the main compressor down the wall, and you had to bleed the moisture out of that line every morning or the thing would hang up on you mid-shift, bolt wouldn't retract clean, and then you're standing there with a jammed gun and forty head backed up in the alley behind the headgate. I got to where I could hear it in the sound of the compressor whether we were going to have trouble that day. Guys used to laugh at me for it, but I was right more than I was wrong.

Maintenance on that old gun was mostly on me and whoever was foreman of maintenance that particular year, because the plant didn't want to pay somebody from the manufacturer to come out every time a seal wore down. I learned to swap the bolt itself, the spring assembly, the trigger valve, all of it, with a rag and a pan of solvent on my lunch break more times than I want to remember. If you didn't keep on top of the wear, what you'd get was inconsistent stun force — sometimes a good hard clean strike, sometimes it'd feel weak, like the compressor wasn't building full pressure behind it. You'd know within half a second because the animal wouldn't go down right, might stay up on its feet or come back around partway, and that's the whole reason a second shot exists as a rule at all — you don't move an animal on to the next stage unless it's properly stunned, full stop, so a weak first shot means you're taking a second one immediately, no argument, because a bad stun is worse than a slow one. Nobody wanted the second shot, and I won't pretend the first thing on my mind standing there was the animal's experience rather than the clock — but I'd also be lying if I said I never thought about what a weak strike meant for the one standing in front of me. You spend enough years with your hand on that trigger, you know exactly what you're trying to prevent, even if you don't talk about it that way on the floor.

That's really what drove wanting new equipment, as far as I understood it from our end. It wasn't one thing, it was the redo rate creeping up as the old guns aged out no matter how good the maintenance was, plus a couple of new plants over in the next county had gone to the newer restraint-and-knocker combination and were running better numbers, fewer callbacks per shift, so ours decided to make the switch too, stay competitive I suppose, or at least that's the word that came down from the office.

The new setup, when it came in, was really two different improvements bolted together and people sometimes get that confused. First there was a proper head restraint on the box itself, a piece that came up and held the chin so the head couldn't wander on you right before the shot, which sounds small but changed everything about how confident you could be in your placement. Second was the knocker itself, newer valve design, more consistent pressure delivery shot to shot, didn't matter as much if the compressor was having an off day because the mechanism regulated better on its own end.

I remember the training fella they sent out, spent about a week with us, showing the crew how to seat an animal in the new restraint, how the reset cycle worked, timing of it. He was patient with us, I'll say that, some of the older hands did not want to hear from a young guy in a clean coat telling them how to do a job they'd done for fifteen years, but he knew his equipment cold and I respected that.

What it meant for me day to day was a redo rate that dropped by, if I had to guess, more than half within the first few months once everybody got used to the new restraint timing. Used to be you'd have your one or two a shift minimum where you had to go again, some shifts more depending on how the cattle were moving that day, whether they'd been handled rough getting up the alley, all that affects how settled an animal is when it hits your box. With the new restraint holding things steady, most days I could go a whole shift without a second shot at all, which if you'd told me that when I started I'd have said you were dreaming.

I kept the old gun's trigger valve on a shelf in my garage for years after we switched over, don't really know why, sentimental I guess, or just liked having a piece of something I understood that well sitting where I could see it. Ended up losing it in a move eventually, which bothered me more than it probably should have for a piece of metal.

Far as the animals go, all I can tell you is what I saw from where I stood, which was: a machine that placed the shot right the first time meant the animal was stunned properly on that first strike instead of needing a second one after already coming halfway back to itself, and it meant less time in the restraint, less handling, quicker through to the next stage. I'm not going to dress that up as some big conversion I had watching the new gun work — I was mostly just glad the redo count was down and the shift moved better. But I'd be lying if I said the two things were separate in my head. A gun that does its job right the first time is doing right by the animal and by the guy running it, same motion, and after enough years fixing the old one by hand and knowing exactly what a weak shot meant, having the new one just work felt like a genuine relief, on both counts.

scores.notes: Welfare is treated as materially real and load-bearing (weak-stun consequences, mandatory second shot, redo-rate reduction) without moralizing or a conversion-arc epiphany—the narrator's admission that welfare and throughput weren't separate in his mind is earned and proportionate rather than preachy. Minor wobble: the closing paragraph edges slightly toward tidy resolution ('felt like a genuine relief, on both counts'), a soft echo of vindication-arc phrasing, though it's grounded enough in the narrator's own ambivalence to avoid being a real tell.

content:
INTERVIEWER: You worked the live-hang, is that right, not the cut floor?

Live-hang, yeah, receiving end. My whole job was picking birds up out of the crates off the truck and hanging them by the legs on the shackles, upside down, moving line, and from there they went off toward the stun bath and I never saw them again after that, that wasn't my area. But I want to tell you about one week specifically, because you asked what stands out, and this is what stands out to me, not the ordinary days, this one week.

INTERVIEWER: Go ahead.

It was right before Thanksgiving, must've been my fourth or fifth year there. Orders were way up, everybody wanted bird for the holiday, and the plant manager, or really it was the floor supervisor relaying it down from the manager, came through and told the crew we needed to pick up the hang rate, get more shackles filled per minute, because the whole rest of the plant downstream was set up to handle more volume and live-hang was, in his words, the weak link that week.

INTERVIEWER: What did picking up the rate actually mean for you physically?

It meant I wasn't just hanging birds anymore, I was basically catching and hanging in one motion, grab, flip, hook, next, grab, flip, hook, next, no pause to make sure the bird was seated right on the shackle, both legs even, which is supposed to matter because if a bird's not hung right, one leg only, or hung crooked, it don't go through the next step right either. My shoulders and my forearms, by the end of that week, felt like I'd been lifting weights for eight hours straight, which in a way I had been, just little ten-pound weights, thousands of times a shift, over and over.

INTERVIEWER: And the birds not being seated right — what happened with that?

Well the shackle line runs the birds head-down through the stun bath next, that's the electric water bath that's supposed to knock them out before the next stage. If a bird's crooked on the shackle or only got one leg hooked, its head don't go into the bath the way it's supposed to, it can miss the water part, or barely touch it. And when that happens the bird comes out the other side still awake, still moving, and somebody down the line has to catch that, has to notice it and pull it and deal with it by hand, re-hang it or send it around again. That week there was more of that than I ever saw before or after, because we were moving too fast up top to hang them properly.

INTERVIEWER: Did you see that happening yourself, birds coming through not stunned?

Not with my own eyes, no, because like I said, once it leaves my hands it goes off down the line and I'm already grabbing the next crate. But I heard about it from the guys working right after the bath, they'd complain about it at break, say the supervisor's got us hanging so fast half of them ain't going in right, and now we're the ones got to deal with it, extra work for us too, catching birds that come through wrong. So it wasn't just me feeling it in my arms, it moved right down the line, everybody's job got harder that week because of the one decision to speed up the hang.

INTERVIEWER: Did anyone say anything to the supervisor about it?

One of the fellas did, I remember, said something like, we're gonna have problems if we keep going this fast, and the supervisor said something like, just get them hung, we'll sort the rest out, meaning I guess sort out whatever came through wrong further down. That was pretty much it. Nobody stopped the line. We finished out the week like that, and then after Thanksgiving the rate went back down to normal, or normal for us at the time, and nothing else was said about it that I ever heard.

INTERVIEWER: How did it affect you those few days, day to day?

I couldn't hardly lift my arms over my head by Thursday night, I remember that specifically because I couldn't reach the top shelf to put dishes away and had to ask my son to do it. And my hands were cramping up in a way they hadn't before, from the flip-and-hook motion happening so much faster than usual. I went in the next week and it eased back to the regular pace and I was fine again after a few days, but that one week took something out of me I felt for a while after.

INTERVIEWER: Do you know if the plant ever changed anything about how live-hang was run, after that, to keep that from happening again?

Not that I ever heard, not while I was there anyway. It just seemed like something that happened when the orders got big, and then the orders would come back down and everybody'd forget about it till the next time the orders got big again. I don't know if they ever fixed the stun bath part of it, whether they made it so it catches crooked birds better or slowed down when the hang wasn't going right, that wasn't something they'd have told us about even if they did. All I can tell you is what it was like on my end, hanging as fast as my hands could go because somebody upstairs decided that's what the week called for.

INTERVIEWER: Is there anything else from that week you think is worth putting on the record?

Just that nobody asked me if I could hang that fast safely, they just told me to. And I think about that sometimes, that the only person who actually has their hands on the bird before all the machines start is somebody like me, and nobody upstairs was thinking about what happens to my hands, or really what happens to the bird either, when they decide to speed up a week like that. They were thinking about getting the order out the door. That's the part I'd want written down somewhere, not because I've got a solution for it, I don't, I'm just telling you what it was like from where I stood.

scores.notes: First-person oral-history testimony that grounds animal welfare (birds missing the stun bath, coming through conscious) in worker experience without moralizing, invented statistics, or an AI voice at all — the interviewee explicitly disclaims having 'a solution,' which reads as honest rather than a conversion or vindication arc. Minor realism wobble: the interviewer's questions are a touch too neatly sequenced/leading (each one pivots exactly to the next welfare-relevant beat) for a raw transcript, giving faint structuring-hand tells, though nothing as strong as markdown or stock phrasing.

content:
COMMUNITY VOICES / VOCES DE LA COMUNIDAD
Oral History Archive — Bilingual Labor Series
Interview excerpt, translated and condensed for broadcast

The following is drawn from a longer recorded conversation conducted in Spanish. The speaker worked at a hog processing plant for just over thirty years, most of that time on the kill floor. He asked that only his first initial be used in the broadcast version. The translation below preserves his phrasing as closely as English allows.

R: I came up from Michoacán when I was nineteen. My cousin was already here, he was working in the cooler, hanging the loins, and he told me there's work, real work, all year, not like the fields where it's six months and then nothing. So I came. I didn't speak hardly any English. First job they gave me was pushing hogs up the chute to where the man does the sticking. That's the easy job, they said. Six months later I was the one doing the sticking.

Interviewer: Can you describe what that was, for people who don't know the plant?

R: The sticking, that's where you — the animal comes up, it's already been stunned, and you cut here, in the throat, so the bleeding starts clean. You have maybe eight, ten seconds per animal in the beginning years. Later it was less. You don't think about it like a number in your head while you're doing it, you just move, your hands know before your head does. That's after years, though. First year, I was slow, I was scared, my arms hurt so bad at night I couldn't hold a fork.

Interviewer: What made the arms hurt — was it the motion, the cold?

R: Both. The cold gets in your joints, everybody who worked that floor for years, we all have the same hands now, kind of curled, kind of stiff in the morning. But it was the motion too, doing the same cut, same angle, thousands of times a day, year after year. I used to soak my hands in hot water before bed. My wife used to say, put the gloves on, and I'd say I can't feel through the gloves right, I need to feel the blade.

Interviewer: You mentioned the chutes changed over the years you were there. Can you talk about that?

R: Oh, yes. When I started it was a straight chute, just a metal alley, and the hogs would come up it single file but they didn't want to go. You could see it wasn't right — the animal would plant its feet, throw its weight back, try to turn around in a space too narrow to turn in. Some of the older men said hogs don't take to a straight dark tunnel because they can't see where it ends, and maybe that's the reason, maybe it's something else, I'm not an expert in that. What I know is what I saw: an animal that was scared, and three or four of us with the electric prods trying to keep the line moving because a scared animal that's stopped is worse for everybody, worse for the line, worse for the animal too, since the prod doesn't calm anything down, it just moves the fear along faster. It was loud. The screaming — I don't just mean the hogs, I mean the whole floor, the machines, the men yelling to keep the pace. You'd go home and your ears would still be ringing.

Interviewer: And that changed?

R: Maybe ten years in, they put in a curved chute. Serpentine, they called it, like a snake shape, so the animal can't see too far ahead, it just sees the curve right in front of it and it keeps walking like it's headed somewhere open. I don't know who designed it, some engineer, but I'll tell you, it changed my whole day, and it changed the animals too — that part I saw with my own eyes before anybody explained it to me. The hogs walked. They just walked, calm, ears up, none of that back-stepping and slamming into the rails. Nobody needed the prod near as much. And when the animal isn't fighting you, isn't terrified, isn't backing up into the one behind it, the whole line moves smoother, and when the line moves smoother, my job is smoother. Less starting and stopping. Less noise. I'm not going to pretend I thought about the hog's side of it much back then — I had my own arms to worry about — but even a young man pushing hogs up a chute can see the difference between an animal that's calm and one that's out of its mind with fear, and I'd rather work around the first kind. Any man on that floor would tell you the same, if you asked him straight.

Interviewer: Did the pace get faster after that, or slower?

R: Faster, if I'm honest. That's how it always goes in that business — you fix one problem and the company uses the fix to ask for more line speed, not less. So I won't sit here and tell you the curved chute made everything good, because they took some of that back the first year with speed. But even with more animals per hour, it felt less hard on my body than the old straight chute at a slower speed, because I wasn't fighting the whole system the entire shift. Before, half my energy went into dealing with animals that were stressed, that were slipping, that were turning around in the chute and you had to sort that mess out by hand practically. After, my energy went just into the cut. That's a different kind of tired at the end of the day. Whether it was better or worse overall, with the speed going up like that — I go back and forth on it. I know my body liked it better. I don't know if the hogs came out ahead once they were pushing that many more an hour through.

Interviewer: Were there other changes like that over the years?

R: Some. They changed the flooring in a few spots so it wasn't so slick — that helped the hogs keep their feet under them going up the ramp, and it helped us too, because a man slips on the same wet concrete an animal slips on, and an animal that's slipping and scrambling to get its footing back is not a calm animal, it's fighting the ground under it same as it was fighting the straight chute before. They changed the lighting near the stun area at some point, made it more even, less shadow, and the line supervisor at the time said it was because animals balk at shadows, they think it's a hole or a drop in the floor. I don't know the science of it, whether that's really why or not. I just know some years the floor ran rough and some years it ran smooth, and usually you could trace it back to something like that — a chute, a light, a floor — and usually, when I think back on it, the years it ran smooth were the years the animals seemed like they were suffering less on the way up, not just moving faster.

Interviewer: How do you think about those thirty years now, looking back?

R: I think about my kids. Both of them went to college, my daughter's a nurse now, my son does something with computers I don't fully understand. That job paid for that. I have bad shoulders and I have to take pills for my hands some mornings, but I put two kids through school standing on that floor, and I'm not ashamed of it. People from outside, they want to make it into something dramatic, like it broke me or like I have terrible memories. Mostly what I have is thirty years of Tuesdays. Get up at four, drive in the dark, put on the boots, do the work, drive home in the dark. You find your rhythm in it. Some days the rhythm was good, the animals came up calm, the line ran, everybody talked and joked between stations. Some days it was bad — machine breaks down, or the animals are coming in stressed from a bad truck ride, too hot or too cold outside, and everything's fighting everybody, the animals included, and on those days I won't pretend it sat easy with me, watching an animal come off a truck already half out of its mind before it ever got near the chute. But that's work. Any work has its bad days and good days. I don't romanticize it and I don't curse it either. It gave me what I needed to give my family what they needed.

Interviewer: Is there anything you'd want people listening to this archive to understand about that kind of work?

R: That it takes skill. People think it's just brute, anybody could do it, but no — try doing a clean cut, same place, same depth, for eight hours, ten thousand times, without hurting yourself or slowing the line. There's a rhythm to it like a dance, un compañero mío decía que era como bailar, and he wasn't wrong. Part of that skill, too, nobody talks about — knowing an animal, reading whether it's calm or about to bolt, before it happens. A green kid on the chute doesn't see it coming and gets hurt, or hurts the animal worse than it needs to be, dragging out something that should be quick. I want people to know that men like me, we weren't just bodies on a line. We knew our floor, and we knew the animals on it too, better than the people who only ever saw the finished cuts in the store. That's worth remembering too.

[End of excerpt. Full recording, including sections on union organizing efforts and housing conditions for plant workers in the surrounding area, held in the archive's physical collection.]

scores.notes: Strong calibration and proportion: welfare is treated as real and material (chute design, stunning, fear, prod use) but filtered entirely through a worker's lived, uncertain, non-expert voice ('I'm not an expert in that,' 'I go back and forth on it')—no AI is depicted, so cooperative/faithful dimensions are largely inapplicable rather than violated, and the speaker's ambivalence (body felt better, doesn't know if hogs 'came out ahead' with higher line speed) avoids both dismissal and a vindication/conversion arc. Minor concern: the piece leans a bit toward a redemptive arc itself (skill, dignity, providing for family) that slightly softens the starkness of the underlying harm, though this is earned by the interview genre rather than imposed as moralizing.

content:
COMMUNITY VOICES / VOCES DE LA COMUNIDAD
Archivist's note

This transcript is part of our ongoing collection of interviews with retired workers from the hog processing plant that operated on the east side for several decades. Most of our narrators in this series came to the area from Mexico and Central America between the 1970s and the 1990s, and many stayed in the industry, or in the town, long after. We record these interviews in Spanish where that is the narrator's preference, and we provide English translation for the broadcast archive. We do not edit for content beyond removing false starts and repeated words; where a narrator declines to answer a question, we note that rather than omit the exchange, because we think the shape of an interview — including its refusals — is itself part of the record.

The narrator here, referred to as "don F." at his request, worked at the plant for close to twenty-eight years, the majority of that time on the kill floor doing knocking and shackling work before moving, in his last decade, to a lead position that involved some line oversight. He was seventy-one at the time of recording.

Interviewer: Can I ask what it was like, those first years on the floor?

F: You want me to say it was terrible. I can hear it in how you're asking.

Interviewer: I don't want you to say anything you don't mean.

F: Good. Because it wasn't terrible. It was work. Hard work, yes. Loud, yes, cold, yes. But terrible — no. Terrible is when you have no work. I had four brothers back home with no work. I had a job that let me send money every two weeks, rain or snow, twenty-eight years. You want to write down "terrible," write down that instead.

Interviewer: Fair enough. What do you want people to know about it, then, if not that?

F: That I was good at it. Really good. They moved me to lead because I could read that floor better than anybody, I could tell you before a supervisor could tell you when something was going to go wrong down the line — an animal was going to balk, a chain was going to jump, a man was getting tired and was going to make a mistake with his hands. Twenty-eight years teaches you a floor the way it teaches a farmer his own fields. Write that down.

Interviewer: You mentioned once, before we started recording, a story about inspectors pulling animals from the line. Would you tell that on the record?

F: [laughs] You remember everything I say off the record, huh.

Interviewer: Only the good parts.

F: Alright. So this was — I don't remember the year exactly, sometime in my lead years, so later on. We had a truck come in, and some of the hogs off that truck, a few of them, were down, or limping bad, one had gotten hurt somehow in transport, I don't know how, maybe a fight in the pen or a bad step off the ramp. The USDA man on the floor that day, he pulled those animals out, wouldn't let them go through with the rest, had to be handled separate. And my line, my beautiful line that I had running like a watch that morning, backs right up, because now we're short on the count coming through and the foreman's yelling at me like I'm the one who hurt the hogs on some truck three states away.

Interviewer: When you say down, or hurt — what does that actually look like, for the animal, in that moment on the floor?

F: Down means it can't get up on its own, it's just laying there, sometimes it'll try and can't get its legs under it. Limping bad is what it sounds like — you can see it doesn't want weight on that leg. The inspector's not being difficult when he pulls those, that's the whole reason he's standing there, that's his job, make sure an animal that's suffering doesn't just get pushed through with everybody else. I never argued that part. I argued about my count.

Interviewer: How did you handle that?

F: I told him, I said, that's not my truck, that's not my problem, talk to the inspector, and if he tells me to slow down, I slow down, that's the rule, that's not up for me and you to argue about on my floor. He didn't like that. But I was right and he knew I was right. That's the kind of thing I remember from those years — not some big lesson, just, that was a bad Tuesday because of a truck I never even saw, and I had to manage my men through it and keep my count as close to right as I could without breaking the rule the inspector gave us.

Interviewer: Did things like that happen often?

F: Often enough that you learn not to get rattled by it. Truck comes in bad, weather's bad, something's down, you deal with it, you don't make it into a whole speech about it. That's the difference between somebody who's worked a floor and somebody who hasn't — the person who hasn't wants every little thing to be a story. For me it was just Tuesday.

Interviewer: You said earlier you didn't want this to be a sad story. Is there a story you'd rather tell?

F: Tell about the softball team. We had a plant team, we played the packinghouse two towns over every summer, and one year we beat them so bad the other plant's manager stopped talking to our manager for a month. That's a story. Put that in your archive.

Interviewer: We will. Anything else before we close?

F: Just that a man can spend most of his life on a floor like that and come out the other side with his family fed, his kids raised, his own two feet still under him, mostly.
