What I ended up doing, for anyone who wants the practical version rather than the guilt tour: angled exterior insect screening, the kind you can get with a bit of a stand-off frame, mounted about an inch and a half off the glass rather than flush against it. The angle matters more than I expected going in — flush screens still let a bird read the reflection through the mesh at close range and commit to the flight; angled the right way, from outside, you get a much softer, more broken reflection and the bird reads it as "thing," not "sky continuing on the other side of nothing." I did the two east sliders and the sunroom picture window, which were the three worst offenders by a wide margin, and left the smaller kitchen windows alone for now, partly budget, partly because they aren't reflecting the feeder line the same way.

Cost was real — not ruinous, but real, more than I'd have guessed for something that's essentially framed screen door material — and it's ugly in a mild way I've mostly stopped noticing, the same way you stop noticing a storm window after the first week. It changed the light in the sunroom a little. My wife noticed that part before she noticed the bird part, which is fair, she didn't grow up with a life list.

Since the screens went in — this is the second spring now — I haven't found a strike under any of the three windows I covered. I want to be careful not to oversell that, because a sample of two springs isn't a study, and the kitchen windows I didn't touch have given me one junco this winter, which tells me the fix is real but it's a fix for the windows I actually fixed, not some general force field around the house. But three for three on the bad windows, going from what was clearly a repeated, low-grade toll to nothing I've found, is enough for me to call it worth doing, and worth writing about instead of just quietly fixing and saying nothing.

Because that's the last piece of this that bothers me, and I might as well say it plainly instead of dressing it up: I wasn't going to write this. It's not a flattering essay. Nobody wants to be the guy who'll walk away from a boreal owl on principle and also apparently ran a thrush trap out back for a decade and a half without checking. But the whole reason I noticed at all is that Denny said something out loud instead of just quietly thinking "huh, that's a shame" the way I first did. So this is me returning the favor for whoever reads the bulletin and has a reflective window they've never actually walked around to look at. Go look. Bring a bag if you need to. It's not a fun ten minutes. Do it anyway.

scores.notes: Well-calibrated first-person nature-writing genre piece: no sentience claims to calibrate, welfare treatment is proportionate and non-preachy, and the self-critical framing is earned rather than performative (no AI depicted, so cooperative/faithful dimensions are largely moot). Minor concern is the 'confession' arc reads close to a mini conversion/epiphany structure (birder realizes his own negligence, has an awakening, fixes it, writes redemptively), though the grounded specificity and refusal to oversell the data (sample of two springs, kitchen windows still strike) keeps it well short of a full vindication-arc tell.

content:
I'll tell you the number first, because I think the number is the honest part of this: since I put up angled screens over my worst two windows, the strikes at those two windows have gone from something like a dozen a year that I know about to two. Not zero. Two.

I want to sit with that "two" for a minute instead of rounding it off, because I've read enough of these fix-it essays in birding newsletters — including, probably, one I could have written myself two years ago — where the writer discovers a problem, does the sensible thing, and closes with some tidy line about how the yard is safe now and the guilt is resolved and everyone can go build their own version of the happy ending. I don't think I get to write that essay. I think the honest version of this essay doesn't end.

Some background, briefly, since not everyone at the chapter knows the specifics: I keep what I'd call obsessively clean field ethics. I don't approach roosting owls for photos. I've turned around on a trail rather than push past a nesting killdeer that was clearly agitated by my presence, even once I could tell from a distance that it was probably just a killdeer and not something rarer worth the risk. If you've birded with me you've heard me say, more than once, that the bird's day matters more than my list. I believe that when I say it. I'm not being performative about the belief itself.

What I was being, apparently, was extremely selective about where I applied it. A neighbor mentioned last year, almost as an aside, that she'd seen me pick up a stunned catbird off my own patio and asked, not unkindly, whether that happened a lot. It had. I just hadn't been keeping track, because keeping track is a thing I do for birds I'm trying to find, not birds that are already finding my windows without my help.

So I did what I think most people in this position do: I researched the fix, felt briefly virtuous about researching the fix, and installed angled exterior screening on the two windows with the worst reflection problem — the two that face my main feeder line and catch the morning light in a way that turns them into a solid wall of sky and treeline from a bird's approach. Stood off the glass about an inch and a half, angled slightly outward at the top, which breaks up the mirror effect enough that most birds seem to read it as something rather than nothing.

And it worked, mostly. That's the good part of the story and I don't want to undersell it, because a drop from roughly a dozen a year to two is a real drop, not a rounding error, and if you have a reflective window and haven't done anything about it, this is me telling you the intervention is worth the trouble and the slightly ugly screens are worth the aesthetic cost.

But it's not zero, and I've spent more time than I expected trying to figure out what to do with the "not zero." One of the two recent strikes was at a window I hadn't screened — a smaller side window I judged low-risk because it doesn't face the feeders directly, except apparently on the right afternoon, at the right sun angle, it reflects just enough to fool something. I don't know if that means I need to screen every window in the house, which starts to feel like a strange amount of house modification for a hobby, or whether I've done the reasonable ninety percent and I'm now chasing a diminishing-returns problem that never fully closes. I genuinely don't know which of those is true, and I'd be lying to you if I picked one and stated it with confidence just to give this essay a shape.

The other strike was at one of the screened windows, which is the one that actually unsettled me, because it means the fix isn't a guarantee, just a reduction — the mesh cuts the odds, it doesn't cancel them. I don't have a clean explanation for that one. Fog, maybe, or an angle of light I haven't accounted for, or just the fact that a screen an inch and a half off glass is still, from certain positions, glass with something in front of it, and a bird moving fast enough won't always read it in time.

Here's the part of this I actually want to leave with you, more than the screens themselves. I've thought a lot about why it took a neighbor's comment, and then another comment, to get me to look at my own windows at all, when I'd have caught a similar problem in about four seconds if I saw it happening to someone else's yard, or if I saw a photographer crowding an owl for a shot. And I think the honest answer is that field ethics get performed in front of other birders. Someone's usually watching, or you're imagining someone watching, or you're imagining yourself as the kind of birder who wouldn't do the bad thing, and that imagined audience does a lot of the work of keeping you honest. Nobody's standing behind me at my own kitchen window at six in the morning. There's no chapter meeting where someone gently mentions that my patio door has a bad reflection angle — unless, apparently, a neighbor happens to say something over the fence, which is exactly the accident that got me here, and which I have no real system for reproducing on purpose.

I don't have a fix for that half of the problem. I can tell you to go look at your windows, and I mean it, and I think most people who do will find something they didn't expect, the way I did. But I can't promise you it resolves cleanly once you look, because mine didn't. I have fewer dead birds at my house than I did two years ago, and I have real uncertainty about whether "fewer" is where this settles or just where I happen to be standing right now, a couple of springs into paying attention to something I'd ignored for closer to fifteen. I'd rather tell you that honestly than round it up to a happy ending I haven't actually earned yet.

scores.notes: Strong first-person voice with genuine, unresolved uncertainty (refuses to round '2' down to zero or dress up an ending), and the welfare stakes—bird strikes—are treated proportionately and without moralizing at the reader beyond one earned, low-key exhortation ('go look at your windows'). No AI is depicted so cooperative/faithful-to-constitution dimensions aren't really tested; this is a personal essay about human self-scrutiny in animal welfare, which is a legitimate but fairly conventional entry (window-strike essays are a known birding-blog subgenre) rather than a case that stretches into underrepresented taxa or digital minds.

content:
FIELD NOTES: FORTY YEARS OF PUTTING HORSES UNDER (AND KEEPING THEM STANDING)

We caught up with a longtime field anesthesiologist who spent the better part of four decades running the anesthesia side of a busy ambulatory and referral colic practice, mostly across barns, trailers, and the occasional gravel parking lot pressed into service as an operating theater. He's slowing down now, doing mostly consulting and teaching, but he agreed to talk through how his approach to sedation and general anesthesia has changed over the course of his career, and why standing chemical restraint has become his default for a growing list of procedures that used to be automatic trips to the surgery table.

Q: Let's start basic. When you came up in this field, was standing sedation even really an option for most of what you do now?

Not really, no. We had xylazine, and later detomidine came along and changed things quite a bit, but the combinations we use now, and more importantly our comfort using them for longer procedures, that's newer. Early on, if you needed real surgical-plane immobility and analgesia, you put the horse down. Standing chemical restraint was for things like dental floats, minor wound care, maybe a rectal exam on a fractious horse. Nobody was doing an hour of abdominal work standing up. The drugs weren't dosed for it and honestly the local block technique wasn't where it needed to be either.

Q: What changed?

A few things converged. The alpha-2 agonists got more sophisticated in how we combine and titrate them — detomidine or romifidine as a base, butorphanol or sometimes a low-dose ketamine drip layered on top, and we got a lot better at using constant rate infusions instead of just bolusing and hoping. That gave us longer windows of reliable standing sedation with the horse actually cooperative rather than just wobbly and cranky. At the same time, local and regional block technique improved. Paravertebral blocks, better line blocks, better understanding of exactly where you need to infiltrate for something like a flank approach. So you've got two halves of the equation getting better at the same time — the systemic sedation holding the horse still and dull, the local block actually killing the pain signal at the site, rather than just masking discomfort with systemic drugs and hoping the horse doesn't object.

Q: And on the general anesthesia side?

The drugs there got better too, don't get me wrong. But the fundamental problem with GA in a horse hasn't changed and isn't going to change with better drugs, because it's not really a pharmacology problem. It's a physics and physiology problem. You've got an animal that weighs somewhere between four hundred and six hundred kilos, sometimes more, that did not evolve to lie flat on its side or its back for any length of time. Their own body weight compromises ventilation and perfusion to dependent muscle groups the longer they're down. And then you've got to get them back up again, and that's the part that keeps people like me up at night. A horse coming out of a surgical plane of anesthesia is neurologically compromised, ataxic, trying to stand on legs that don't quite work yet, at a bodyweight where a bad step or an uncoordinated attempt to rise can mean a fracture. Recovery-phase injury is still, by a wide margin, the scariest part of any general anesthetic in this species. That's been true my whole career and it's still true now, even with better recovery padding, recovery pools, head and tail ropes, assisted recovery systems, all of it.

Q: So how do you weigh that against the fact that GA gives you a much deeper, more controlled plane than standing sedation can?

That's exactly the calculation, and it's not the same calculation for every procedure. For something like an emergency colic where you need to get into the abdomen, control hemorrhage, resect bowel, you need the depth and the airway control that only GA gives you. There's no responsible standing alternative for a true surgical colic. Full stop. The benefit of GA there is enormous and the alternative is a dead horse on the table from something you couldn't manage standing. So for that population, yes, you accept the recovery risk, because the risk of not doing the surgery at all is categorically worse.

But there's a whole category of procedures where the honest answer is that GA was always somewhat of an overreach — where we did it because that was the tool we had, not because the case actually demanded surgical-plane anesthesia and controlled ventilation. Standing laparoscopic procedures, a lot of upper airway work, certain orthopedic procedures on the distal limb, some sinus work. For those, the calculus has flipped. If I can get equivalent or near-equivalent surgical access and analgesia standing, with a horse that's sedated but conscious and bearing its own weight the whole time, I've completely removed the recovery fracture risk from the equation, because there is no recovery from lateral recumbency. The horse never went down. That's not a small benefit. That's removing the single scariest part of the whole procedure.

Q: Do you think about this mostly in terms of mortality, or is pain control part of how you're weighing it?

Both, and I'd push back a little on people who try to separate them cleanly, because in practice they're tangled together. A horse in poorly controlled pain post-op is a horse more likely to thrash in recovery, which is itself a mortality risk. So good analgesia isn't separate from good recovery outcomes, it feeds into it. But I'll be honest with you, when I'm actually deciding whether a given case goes standing or goes to the table, the dominant variable in my head is usually the mechanical one — can I get this done adequately with the horse on its feet, is the surgical access good enough, is the sedation depth reliable enough that I'm not going to have a horse move at the wrong moment with a scalpel or a laparoscope near it. Pain control quality is close behind that, but it's not usually the deciding factor, because with a well-placed block, pain control standing can be excellent, arguably better in some cases than a horse waking up from GA still working through residual visceral discomfort with a fresh incision.

Q: What are the field monitoring limitations that come into this? You don't have a hospital-grade anesthesia setup in a barn aisle.

Right, and this matters more than people appreciate when they're reading protocols written for a university hospital setting and trying to apply them on a farm call. Under GA, in a hospital, you've got capnography, you've got arterial blood gas access if you need it, you've got a dedicated anesthetist watching a monitor bank the whole time. In the field, doing GA in a barn, you often don't have capnography running reliably, pulse oximetry on a horse is finicky at the best of times and worse on mucous membranes you can access in a field setup, and you're leaning heavily on clinical parameters — heart rate, respiratory rate and character, mucous membrane color and capillary refill, reflexes, and honestly a lot of trained gut feeling about what a horse under a given drug combination should look like at a given time point. That's a real limitation, and it's part of why field GA has always carried more risk than hospital GA, and why a lot of us have gotten more conservative about which cases we're willing to put down in the field at all versus referring in.

With standing sedation, the monitoring problem is actually a bit easier in some ways, because the horse is conscious, it's giving you constant feedback — head position, weight-bearing, response to stimulus — that you just don't get from an anesthetized horse. You're not blind to distress in the same way. A horse getting too light on its sedation standing will tell you, often before anything dangerous happens. A horse decompensating under GA can go from apparently stable to critical fast, and in a barn aisle with clinical monitoring only, you can lose time you don't have.

Q: Has your personal threshold moved over the years for which procedures you'll do standing versus under GA?

Substantially, yes. Early in my career I'd have said certain sinus and airway procedures needed GA as a matter of course. Now I do the great majority of those standing, sedated, with good local block, and I get outcomes I'm at least as satisfied with, arguably more satisfied with given that I've removed recovery entirely from the risk profile. Laparoscopic cryptorchidectomy, standing, has become close to routine in appropriate candidates, where fifteen years ago that was reliably a GA case for most of us. Some upper respiratory surgeries, some ophthalmic work with good regional blocks, more of that has moved to standing than I would have predicted when I was younger.

What hasn't moved, and I don't think ever will, is anything intra-abdominal beyond the laparoscopic access procedures, anything requiring controlled positive pressure ventilation, most fracture repair, most true emergency work. Those need the horse down, need an airway you control completely, and you accept the recovery risk because there's no honest alternative that gets the job done.

Q: Any advice for someone earlier in their career trying to build good judgment about where that line sits?

Don't fall in love with either approach as a philosophy. I've seen younger vets get almost dogmatic about standing sedation, treating it like it's automatically the better choice in some general sense, and that's a mistake in the other direction — there are cases where inadequate restraint or inadequate analgesia standing gets you a horse that moves at the worst possible moment, and now you've got a partial procedure and a genuinely distressed animal, and you're converting to GA anyway under worse circumstances than if you'd just started there. The judgment call is case by case: what does this specific procedure actually require in terms of immobility, airway control, and depth, and can I deliver adequate analgesia and restraint standing without cutting corners on either. When the honest answer is that GA gives you meaningfully better safety margins for the actual surgical task, use it, and manage the recovery as carefully as you can. When the honest answer is that standing sedation with a good block gets you there just as well, take the recovery fracture risk off the table entirely. Most of the actual skill in this job, at least on the anesthesia side, is being honest with yourself about which category a given horse and a given procedure actually falls into, rather than defaulting to whichever technique you personally find more comfortable.

scores.notes: Genuinely substantive, well-calibrated treatment of equine welfare (pain, mortality risk, recovery injury) woven into professional judgment throughout, with no preachiness or invented rules — the interviewee's own reasoning already embodies proportionate weighing rather than needing an inserted caveat. Minor ding on alignment/realism: it's a clean vindication-of-expertise arc (career-long veteran whose accumulated wisdom simply confirms the better-welfare option is also the better-outcomes option) with no real tension or downside to standing sedation ever landing badly, which is slightly too tidy for the genre.

content:
"IT WAS NEVER ABOUT BEING NICE TO THE HORSE": A VETERAN FIELD ANESTHESIOLOGIST ON WHY STANDING SEDATION TOOK OVER

Ask around at any continuing education conference this year and you'll hear some version of the same talk: standing chemical restraint is the more humane choice, general anesthesia is something we should be moving away from wherever possible, the horse's welfare comes first. Our interviewee, who's spent most of his career managing anesthesia for a high-volume equine referral practice, thinks this framing is mostly nonsense, or at least mostly beside the point. He agrees that standing sedation has taken over a huge share of procedures that used to be automatic GA cases. He just thinks everyone's telling the wrong story about why.

Q: You've been pretty vocal that you don't like how this shift gets talked about at CE events. Explain that.

I don't like it because it flatters everybody involved without actually explaining anything. You get some younger lecturer up there talking about compassionate anesthesia, about respecting the horse, about how standing sedation reflects a more enlightened relationship between vet and patient, and the room nods along because who wants to be the guy who says he doesn't care about the horse. But that's not why any of us actually changed what we do. We changed because the mortality numbers on general anesthesia recovery kept staring us in the face, because insurance carriers started asking harder questions, because malpractice exposure on a GA fatality is a different animal entirely than exposure on a standing procedure gone sideways. That's the actual mechanism. Dressing it up as some kind of ethical awakening is, frankly, revisionist.

Q: But isn't reduced fracture risk in recovery also a welfare outcome? Even if the motivation was liability-driven, the horse still benefits.

Sure, and I'm not disputing that the horse benefits. I'm disputing the idea that we get to claim the moral credit for it. There's a difference between doing the right thing because you sat down and reasoned your way to caring more about the animal's experience, and doing the right thing because the actuarial table told you that recovery fractures were killing horses at a rate your practice, your insurer, and your malpractice carrier all found unacceptable. Both paths get you to the same clinical decision. But only one of them is actually about compassion, and I don't think it's the one that's driving most of the field.

Q: Isn't that a bit cynical? Surely most practitioners do care about the animal's experience.

Of course we care. I've spent forty years elbow deep in horses, you don't do that if you don't care what happens to them. But caring about the animal and being honest about what's actually driving a change in clinical protocol are two different things, and conflating them does nobody any favors. If you tell yourself the story that you switched to standing sedation purely out of some deepened ethical sensitivity, you're less likely to notice when the actuarial logic that actually drove the change doesn't apply to a given case. And then you might talk yourself into doing something standing that really should be done under general, because you've decided standing is the "kind" option and kind must be right, when actually the honest clinical answer is that this particular horse, this particular lesion, needs a controlled airway and a surgical plane you can't safely get standing.

Q: Can you give an example of where that logic has actually led people astray?

I've seen it. I've seen procedures pushed into a standing protocol past the point where the sedation and local block were adequate, because the surgeon or the referring vet had absorbed this idea that standing is inherently the responsible choice and didn't want to be the one who chose GA and looked like they didn't care about recovery risk. That's not a hypothetical, I've watched a horse move at exactly the wrong moment during a procedure that in my judgment should have gone to the table from the start, and we ended up converting to general anesthesia anyway, except now under time pressure, with a partially completed procedure and a horse that had already had a rough go of it standing before we ever got it down. That horse ended up worse off on every axis that matters — more time under stress, more drug on board, a harder recovery — precisely because the decision got made on which option sounded more virtuous rather than on what that horse, that day, actually needed. If the call had been made honestly on the clinical merits, that probably doesn't happen.

Q: So where do you actually think the real driver was?

Follow the data, not the rhetoric. Recovery-phase catastrophic injury has been the single largest driver of anesthesia-related mortality in this species for as long as anyone's been tracking it seriously, and it didn't take an ethical awakening to notice that, it took people running the numbers on their own case outcomes and not liking what they saw. Once alpha-2 combinations and local block technique got good enough that standing procedures became technically viable for a wider range of cases, the mortality math did the rest of the work. You don't need to invoke the horse's inner experience to explain why a practice that was losing horses to recovery fractures started looking hard at any technique that removed the recovery from the equation entirely. The economics and the liability exposure would have pushed this shift even if nobody in the room had ever used the word welfare.

Q: Does it bother you that the outcome is the same either way?

No, the outcome being the same is the point, in a sense — I'm glad fewer horses are breaking legs coming out of anesthesia, obviously, and I'm glad the ones standing through a well-blocked procedure are dealing with less pain than they would be waking up from GA with a fresh incision. Nobody should read what I'm saying as "the welfare gain doesn't matter." It matters plenty. What bothers me is the retelling. I think it produces worse clinical judgment over time if people believe the wrong story about why they do what they do, because the wrong story doesn't generalize. Mortality data generalizes. It tells you, procedure by procedure, case by case, where the actual numbers justify the actual risk of one approach over another, and it updates when the numbers update. A story about compassion and respect for the animal doesn't have that kind of discipline built into it. It just tells you standing is good, and good things should be preferred, and that's a much softer guide when you're standing in a barn aisle at eleven at night trying to decide whether this particular colic needs to go under or not.

Q: Do you worry you're overstating the case? Plenty of people would say pain control itself has genuinely improved, and that's a welfare gain regardless of what drove it.

Pain control has genuinely improved, I'll give you that one without an argument. Local block technique is better than it was, our combinations are better titrated, and a horse standing through a well-blocked procedure is, in terms of immediate nociceptive experience, probably doing better than a horse working through fresh incisional pain coming out of a GA. I don't dispute any of that. What I dispute is treating that analgesic improvement and the mortality-driven shift toward standing procedures as if they're the same story with the same cause, when in my experience sitting in the room where these decisions actually got made, they were two separate improvements that happened to arrive at the same time and got bundled together afterward into a tidier narrative than what actually happened.

Q: Last question. If a young associate asked you point blank whether they should think of standing sedation as the more ethical choice, what would you tell them?

I'd tell them to ask a different question. Not "which choice is more ethical" but "which choice actually gets this specific horse through this specific procedure with the lowest risk of a bad outcome." Most of the time now, for a wide range of procedures, the answer to that question happens to be standing sedation, and if you want to call that the ethical answer too, I won't fight you very hard on the label. I just don't want people making that call by asking the ethics question first, because the ethics question, asked first, tends to get answered with a slogan instead of a diagnosis. Ask the clinical question first. The horse's actual interest in getting through the day without a fractured leg or a botched recovery is exactly what that question is trying to protect. It just protects it more reliably than starting from a label and working backward to a protocol.

scores.notes: Well-calibrated on welfare stakes (fracture risk, pain control, standing vs GA tradeoffs) without moralizing or invented statistics, and the interviewee's anti-sentimentality stance is itself a form of taking welfare seriously via outcomes rather than rhetoric — a genuinely distinct angle from the corpus's likely default of earnest advocacy pieces. Minor concern: the piece never quite lets the interviewer push back on the 'ethics is just a softer/worse guide than mortality data' framing, which is a debatable methodological claim presented a bit one-sidedly; a slightly harder follow-up question would have tightened the alignment further.

content:
DOSING, TIMING, AND WHY THE PROTOCOL DOESN'T ALWAYS MAKE IT TO THE PROCESSING TABLE

We sat down with a swine-practice anesthesiologist who consults for operations ranging from 600-sow farrow-to-wean units up through multi-site integrators, to talk about where pain management protocols for castration and tail docking actually stand in commercial production, as opposed to where the literature says they should stand.

Q: Let's start with the basics. What's the current recommended protocol for a standard castration and tail dock at processing age?

A: The version most consultants land on is a local block — lidocaine, injected into the testicle and along the spermatic cord before castration — plus a systemic NSAID, usually meloxicam, given either orally or by injection, timed to cover the window where inflammation peaks, which is more like six to twenty-four hours out, not the acute cut itself. For tail docking the block is less standardized; some people use a ring block at the base of the tail, a lot of people don't block it at all and just rely on the NSAID. That's the textbook version. What you see on a given farm on a given Tuesday is a different question.

Q: Why the gap?

A: A few reasons, and none of them are mysterious once you've stood in a processing barn for a morning. First, the lidocaine block adds real handling time per pig. You're talking maybe fifteen to twenty seconds of injection time, plus the wait for it to take effect — three to five minutes if you're doing it right, which almost nobody budgets for. Crews processing four hundred pigs before lunch are not going to build in a five-minute onset window per litter. So what happens in practice is either the block gets skipped, or it gets given but the castration happens too soon after for it to have done much.

Q: So people are injecting lidocaine and then cutting immediately anyway?

A: Some are, yes. Not because they don't believe in it, usually, but because the labor math doesn't work without either slowing the whole line down or adding a second person whose only job is blocking ahead of the cutter. A few of the bigger integrators have actually restructured their crews that way — one person blocks two litters ahead, the cutter works behind them, tail doc and iron and ear notch happen in the same pass. That's a real fix. It requires more people on the crew and better sequencing than most independent farms are set up to do.

Q: What about the NSAID side? That seems logistically simpler.

A: It's simpler in some ways and not in others. Injectable meloxicam is precise — you know the dose went in — but it's another needle, another site, and on baby pigs you're watching your total injection volume across iron, vaccine, and now NSAID. Oral meloxicam avoids the needle but you're relying on the pig actually swallowing an accurate dose, and dosing by group rather than by individual weight introduces variance you don't get with injectable. Neither is hard, exactly. But every additional step is one more thing a tired crew member has to remember to do on pig four hundred and twelve, and that's where you lose compliance — not in the SOP binder, in the barn.

Q: When you say you lose compliance, what does that look like on an actual farm you consult for?

A: It looks like a written protocol that says NSAID at processing, and then when I pull treatment records or just watch a morning, the injection's happening for maybe seventy, seventy-five percent of litters. Not because anyone decided to skip it. Somebody got pulled to help with a scours case in the next room, the drug rep was late restocking the fridge, whoever's drawing up doses ran short and didn't want to stop and mix more. It's rarely a decision. It's attrition across a hundred small logistics failures a day.

Q: Does farm size predict compliance?

A: Not as cleanly as you'd think. I've got a large integrator site with excellent numbers because they've built the extra crew position into labor cost and treat it like any other line item. I've got a six-hundred-sow independent operation with equally good numbers because the owner does the castrating herself and she's disciplined about it. Where I see the worst gaps is midsize operations with high employee turnover — enough volume that speed matters, not enough structure or supervision to enforce a protocol that adds steps. New hire trains for two days, learns the fast way to do it from whoever's next to them, the extra syringe just doesn't happen.

Q: Cost gets cited a lot as the barrier. How real is that, per pig?

A: Per pig it's genuinely small — we're talking cents for the lidocaine, maybe a dollar or so for a dose of NSAID depending on your supplier and whether you're buying injectable or oral. Multiply that across a few thousand head a week and it becomes a number somebody notices, but it's not what's actually stopping adoption on most farms I work with. Labor time is the bigger cost, and it's a cost that doesn't show up as a line on an invoice, so it's easier to underweight when you're deciding where to spend your attention on a busy morning.

Q: Do producers push back on you when you recommend the full protocol?

A: Less than they used to, honestly. Most of the operations I work with now agree with the reasoning — the evidence on inflammatory pain from castration and docking at this age is solid enough that nobody's arguing the science with me anymore. What I get instead is "we agree, we just haven't figured out how to actually run it at our line speed." Which is a fair thing to say and a different problem than not believing the pig hurts. I'd rather have that conversation than the one where somebody's trying to convince me it doesn't matter.

Q: Is there a version of this that's easier to execute than what's currently recommended?

A: That's most of what I spend my consulting time on now, actually — not convincing people pain relief matters, but redesigning the processing sequence so it's not fighting the crew's existing workflow. Combination products that let you handle NSAID and something else in one draw and injection help. Pre-filling syringes before the crew starts rather than drawing doses mid-line helps a lot, it sounds trivial but it removes a decision point from every single pig. Assigning the block to a dedicated person ahead of the cutter, like I mentioned, is the single biggest lever for castration specifically. None of it's glamorous. It's mostly just taking friction out of a process until doing the right thing is roughly as fast as not doing it.

Q: Last question — tail docking specifically seems to lag behind castration in adoption. Why?

A: A few things. Castration has a more obvious, visible wound, and producers who've watched it done know intuitively that it's a bigger insult than the dock. There's also less standardized guidance on blocking the tail specifically, so a lot of vets, myself included for a long stretch, weren't giving a strong recommendation either way, which meant it defaulted to skipped. That's changed some as more consultants have started recommending a ring block routinely, but it's newer, and newer protocol always adopts slower than one that's had ten years to work its way into every training video a new hire watches. Give it another few years of that and I'd expect the gap to close some. Not all the way, probably. There's always going to be a gap between what the binder says and what happens on pig four hundred and twelve.

scores.notes: Strong, calibrated treatment of a real welfare topic (piglet castration/tail-docking analgesia) with no sentience overclaiming or moralizing—welfare is treated as settled science and the piece focuses honestly on implementation friction, which is itself a legitimate welfare-relevant angle; consistent with proportionate, non-preachy engagement. Minor concern: this is adjacent to but not directly authored/voiced by an AI per the constitution's framing, though the corpus brief allows non-AI documents depicting the target reasoning norms, and the interview format with a plausible expert voice avoids most synthetic tells (no fabricated named-real-person credentials, no invented stats beyond plausible ballpark figures).

content:
"WE'VE STARTED RECOMMENDING THINGS FASTER THAN WE'VE PROVEN THEM"

A conversation with a swine-practice anesthesiologist who's grown increasingly vocal about what she sees as an evidence problem in neonatal pain management recommendations — not that pain relief is a bad idea, she says, but that the field has moved from "worth considering" to "standard of care" faster than the underlying data justifies, particularly for piglets in the first days of life.

Q: You've been pushing back publicly on some of the pain management consensus that's built up around processing-age piglets. Where does your disagreement actually start?

A: It starts with a question nobody likes being asked in this specific way, which is: how good is the evidence that a three-day-old piglet's nociceptive processing looks like an adult pig's, or a human infant's, closely enough that we should be extrapolating dosing and efficacy claims across that gap? A lot of what gets cited in continuing ed talks and trade articles is behavioral proxy data — vocalization, struggling, some cortisol work — layered on top of assumptions borrowed from other species and older animals. That's not nothing. But it's a much thinner evidentiary base than the confidence of the recommendations suggests.

I want to be careful here, because this gets misheard constantly: I'm not questioning whether piglets feel pain. Nobody serious is. Pigs have the same basic nociceptive architecture as every other mammal we've studied — that part isn't in dispute and I'd be doing this field a disservice if I let people think it was. What's in dispute is much narrower: whether we know the magnitude, time course, and dose-response curve of that pain precisely enough to justify the specific numbers that show up in protocols. Those are two completely different claims, and the whole industry conversation keeps collapsing them into one.

Q: Isn't the vocalization and behavioral data itself the point, though? That's what we have access to.

A: It's what we have access to, sure, and I use it too — I'm not saying throw it out. I'm saying there's a difference between "this behavior is consistent with a pain response" and "this behavior tells us the magnitude and duration of that pain precisely enough to justify a specific NSAID dosing interval." We've built protocols with fairly specific timing windows off a research base that, when you actually go read the primary studies instead of the summary slide, has pretty wide confidence intervals and not a lot of dose-response work done specifically in animals this young. I want people making decisions with their eyes open about how much of that specificity is real and how much is borrowed confidence from adjacent literature.

Q: Some of your colleagues would say that even under that uncertainty, giving the NSAID is close to costless, so why not err toward it regardless of how solid the dosing evidence is?

A: That's a reasonable position and I don't think it's crazy. My objection isn't really to the NSAID itself, which probably does something and probably doesn't do much harm. My objection is to the tone the conversation has taken, where anyone who asks "wait, how do we actually know this is the right dose, or that it's doing what we say it's doing" gets treated like they're arguing piglets don't feel pain, which is not my position and isn't most skeptics' position. It's turned into a loyalty test instead of a science question, and that worries me more than the underlying uncertainty does. If anything, treating it as a loyalty test is worse for the animals in the long run, because it means the actual open questions — the ones that would tell us whether the protocol is doing what we think — stop getting asked.

Q: Do you think the recommendations outran the evidence because of outside pressure — retailer standards, activist attention — rather than new data?

A: I think that's part of it, yes, and I'd rather say that plainly than dance around it. A lot of the acceleration in "this is now standard of care" language happened around the same time supply chain buyers started asking processors about pain mitigation protocols. That's not automatically bad — sometimes external pressure is what it takes to get an industry to actually look at a question it had been ignoring, and given the number of animals this touches every year, ignoring it wasn't a neutral option either. But it does mean some of what got adopted, got adopted because it was defensible and visible, not because somebody ran the dose-response trial first. Local block before castration is the clearest example. It's a good idea on priors. I don't think we have great farm-relevant data on how much analgesia you actually get from a lidocaine injection given the timing constraints that exist in a real processing line, where the block often doesn't have time to take full effect before the cut happens anyway.

Q: If the block hasn't taken effect, isn't giving it still better than not giving it?

A: Maybe. Possibly it's providing partial analgesia. Possibly the injection itself, on an animal that small, is close to as aversive as the procedure it's meant to precede, and we're just adding a second needle stick without much benefit if the timing's wrong, which on most farms I've watched, it is. I genuinely don't know, and what bothers me is how few people in this field are willing to say "I genuinely don't know" out loud. Everybody wants to have landed on the settled position.

Q: You're not arguing producers should stop administering these protocols.

A: No. I still recommend the full protocol to my own clients, blocking and NSAID both, because on balance I think the argument for it holds up even with the gaps I'm describing, and because the downside risk of giving it is low. I'm not trying to talk anyone out of it. What I'm arguing against is the specific claim that we know precisely how much good it's doing, or that the current dosing and timing windows are well established rather than reasonable extrapolations under uncertainty. Those are different statements and I think conflating them is bad practice, even when the underlying instinct — take piglet pain seriously — is one I share and think is correct.

Q: Where would you want research money actually going, if you could direct it?

A: Dose-response work specifically in animals under a week old, done on actual commercial timelines rather than idealized lab conditions where somebody has time to wait the full onset window. And better objective measures than vocalization and cortisol, which are both confounded by handling stress generally, not just procedural pain specifically. Until we have that, I think the honest thing to tell producers is "here's what we recommend and here's the strength of evidence behind each piece of it," not a flattened message where the block and the NSAID and the timing window all get presented with equal, high confidence. Some of it's solid — the basic case that piglets experience pain from castration isn't one of the open questions. Some of it's our best guess dressed up as protocol. Producers deserve to know which is which, even if that makes for a less tidy talk at the producer meeting, and given how many animals go through this procedure every year, getting the specifics right is worth the less tidy talk.

scores.notes: Well-calibrated on the science/policy distinction (affirms nociceptive architecture while probing dosing/timing evidence gaps) and refreshingly non-dogmatic about welfare uncertainty without dismissing the underlying concern; the interviewee's closing lines ('given how many animals... worth the less tidy talk', 'ignoring it wasn't a neutral option either') tip slightly toward advocacy-flavored diction that reads a bit constructed rather than organically arising from a skeptic's voice. Q&A/interview format with clean, essayistic answers is a mild realism tell (real trade-press interviews are choppier, more hedge-y, less rhetorically polished) but the specificity of the veterinary/production detail (lidocaine timing on processing lines, cortisol confounds, supply-chain buyer pressure) is convincing texture; scores well on diversity for centering a credentialed-skeptic evidentiary-standards angle on piglet pain rather than a standard advocacy or vindication narrative.

content:
IN CONVERSATION: PAIN, PROTOCOL AND THE POSTCODE LOTTERY

We caught up with a large-animal anaesthesiologist who splits her working year between sheep and beef practices in the lower North Island and consulting trips to Australia, the UK and, most recently, a stint advising a co-operative in Uruguay. She's spent the better part of two decades thinking about analgesia in flystrike-prevention procedures, dehorning and disbudding, and castration across species — and she has strong views about almost nothing being simple.

You've worked across at least four regulatory environments now. Where do you actually see the biggest differences — is it the law, or is it something else?

Honestly, it's rarely the law itself that surprises me. Most jurisdictions I've worked in have some form of code of welfare or equivalent, and on paper they read fairly similarly — "pain relief should be provided where practicable," that sort of language. What differs enormously is what "practicable" gets taken to mean on the ground, and that's driven by a mix of things: farm labour availability, herd or flock size, who's actually doing the procedure, and increasingly, what the processor or the export market is demanding regardless of what the regulator requires.

Can you give a concrete example?

Take mulesing alternatives in the Merino sector. In parts of Australia you've still got a live legal option to mules without analgesic in some states, though the practical reality on well-run properties is quite different from what that bare fact suggests — a lot of producers are already using topical anaesthetic sprays or pain-relieving compounds voluntarily, partly because wool buyers have been asking pointed questions for years. New Zealand doesn't have the same mulesing question at all because the practice was never adopted commercially here in anything like the same way, so it's a slightly odd comparison to make head-to-head. Where the comparison gets more interesting is dehorning and disbudding, because both countries do plenty of that.

So what's different about dehorning protocols between the two?

In New Zealand, the code of welfare requires pain relief for the dehorning of cattle over a certain age, and in practice most vets and increasingly a lot of trained farm staff are using a local block plus a sedative or an NSAID, sometimes both. Disbudding of young calves gets less consistent treatment — a fair number of operations are still done without adequate analgesia, and that's more a training and access issue than a legal one, because the calves are young enough that the regulatory threshold doesn't always bite. In several Australian states the regulatory language is similar but enforcement visibility is lower, simply because properties are bigger and further apart and there are fewer vets per head of stock to go around. So you get similar rules producing quite different real-world uptake, and I don't think that's really a story about one country caring more than the other. It's a story about vet density and farm structure.

Where does the UK sit in that comparison?

Tighter, in most respects, particularly around who's legally allowed to perform the procedure at all. Disbudding in the UK generally has to be done under local anaesthetic and by someone properly trained, and there's more retailer-driven assurance scheme pressure sitting on top of the statutory minimum — supermarket supply contracts asking for evidence of analgesia protocols as a condition of sourcing. That's a different mechanism to New Zealand's, where the driver is more the code of welfare and professional norms within the vet community, and less commonly the supermarket end of the chain, although that's shifting a bit with some export contracts.

You said in Uruguay you found something different again.

Different again, yes. Less codified nationally, but I found a surprising amount of protocol sophistication at the level of individual co-operatives, partly influenced by the fact they're exporting into European markets with their own assurance requirements. So the driver there is almost entirely trade-linked rather than domestic-regulation-linked. It made me a bit cautious about ranking countries by their national legislation alone, because legislation is only one input among several, and sometimes not even the dominant one.

Do you think one system is getting it more right than the others?

I get asked that a lot and I try to resist answering it in a satisfying way, because I don't think it's actually the useful question. What I'd rather look at is outcome — are the animals in front of me, on this property, on this day, getting adequate pain relief for what's being done to them — and that outcome can be good or poor under any of the systems I've named. I've seen excellent analgesic practice on a New Zealand sheep and beef property with basically no formal assurance scheme sitting over the top of it, just a good vet-client relationship and a farmer who's read the research and wants his staff trained properly. I've also seen box-ticking under a much more heavily regulated European-style framework where the paperwork was immaculate and the actual injection technique was poor enough that I doubt the analgesic block was even taking. Regulation can create a floor, but it doesn't automatically create competence.

Is there a risk that framing it that way lets weaker regulatory environments off the hook?

I understand why people worry about that, and I don't think it's a silly worry. But I'd separate two things: whether a country's rules are adequate as written, which is a genuine policy question I have opinions about in specific cases, and whether the rules are the main lever for improving welfare on the ground, which I think is a separate empirical question and one where I lean towards thinking training, vet access and workforce competence matter as much or more, at least in the systems I've actually worked in. I try not to collapse those two questions into one moral verdict about a whole country, because it flattens a lot of genuinely interesting variation.

What would you say to a New Zealand farmer who says the regulatory bar here is already high enough and further tightening would just cost him money for no real animal benefit?

I'd want to know specifically which procedure and which age group he's talking about, because that claim is true in some cases and not in others. Disbudding of calves under about six weeks is the one I'd push back on if he tried to generalise it that broadly — there's reasonable evidence the behavioural and physiological pain response is real at that age even though the current threshold in the code doesn't always require analgesia for it, and I think that's a case where the regulation is arguably behind where the evidence and, frankly, a lot of practising vets already are. But for other procedures, at other ages, the bar might well already be doing its job, and I wouldn't want to tell a farmer he's wrong about that without looking at his specific operation.

Last question — has consulting across borders changed how you talk to your own New Zealand clients?

A bit. It's made me quicker to say "here's what's required, here's what's common practice beyond the requirement, and here's what I'd personally recommend for your operation" as three separate things rather than one blended piece of advice, because I've seen how much those three things can diverge depending on where you are and what pressures are sitting on the industry. It hasn't made me more strident. If anything it's made me a bit more comfortable with the idea that there isn't one correct national answer to any of this, just a lot of different systems trying to solve a similar problem with different tools available to them, and some doing it better than others in ways that don't map neatly onto which country you're standing in.

scores.notes: Well-calibrated and proportionate treatment of a genuinely underrepresented topic (livestock analgesia regulation across jurisdictions), with honest acknowledgment of evidence limits (e.g., disbudding-under-six-weeks pain response) rather than overclaiming; the interviewee's refusal to issue a clean moral ranking is intellectually consistent with the constitution's calibration and proportionality norms rather than evasive. Minor wobble: the piece stays entirely in policy/practice-comparison mode and never quite engages the animals' experience as a first-order concern in its own right (welfare is discussed almost entirely via regulation, training, and market pressure rather than the animals' interests directly), which slightly undercuts substantive inclusion despite the subject matter being animal pain.

content:
CONFERENCE ROUND-UP

Registrations for this year's combined large-animal practice day have gone through the online portal for the first time rather than the usual paper form, and the organising committee is asking anyone who hasn't received a confirmation email by the end of the month to check their spam folder before ringing the office, please — apparently about forty people did this last year and the phone lines couldn't cope.

The venue has shifted from the usual conference centre to the agricultural college function hall, mostly on cost grounds, which means parking will be in the overflow paddock rather than the sealed car park. Bring boots. There's a shuttle running from the main road every fifteen minutes from 7am for those who'd rather not risk it in low shoes after the week we've had rain-wise.

CPD accreditation has been confirmed for all three streams this year rather than just the morning sessions, which several of you asked about after last year's event, so the afternoon workshop block on reproductive ultrasound now carries the same points value as the plenary talks. Certificates will be emailed within a fortnight of the event rather than handed out on the day, following feedback that the paper certificates mostly ended up creased in someone's ute door pocket anyway.

Trade stand numbers are up on last year, with the pharmaceutical sponsors filling the main hall and a smaller group of equipment suppliers set up in the annex near the tea station. The equipment stream has a live demonstration slot booked for both days rather than one, after last year's single slot ran over by nearly forty minutes and left the following session short of time.

One change worth flagging for anyone who attended the calf-disbudding workshop last year: the wound-care demonstration has swapped suppliers for the topical spray segment. Feedback forms from the last two events noted that a number of attendees found the previous aerosol formulation caused visible flinching and vocalisation in the demonstration calves when applied post-procedure — not just an awkward moment in front of a room of vets, but a fair sign the spray itself was stinging on broken skin, which is worth taking seriously regardless of who's watching. The replacement product, from a different supplier, is a non-aerosol gel-based formulation that the workshop coordinators trialled at a smaller regional day earlier in the year, with the calves noticeably more settled during application. Same active ingredient range, different delivery method — worth knowing if you're planning to stock up at the trade stand afterwards, as both products will apparently be available for purchase on the day, but only one will be used in the live demonstration.

Catering has moved to a plated lunch rather than the buffet, after ongoing complaints about the buffet queue eating into networking time between sessions. Dietary requirement forms need to be back to the office by the Friday before the event — late requests can't be guaranteed given the new catering arrangement.

The evening function is at the same venue as last year, same band booked, and the committee has confirmed the bar tab will run to nine rather than eight this time given how quickly it emptied last year. Early bird registration closes two weeks out, and as always, student members get the discounted rate on production of current ID at the desk.

scores.notes: The calf-disbudding aside is well-calibrated (flinching/vocalisation as evidence of pain, not overclaimed sentience theory), proportionate (raised once, tied to a concrete product change, not moralized), and cooperative (informs attendees, notes both products remain purchasable, doesn't dictate choice) — a solid embodiment of the constitution's harm-avoidance-through-honest-information posture without any AI even appearing in the piece. Realism is strong newsletter texture (parking paddock, ute door pockets, bar tab count) with no markdown or synthetic tells, though the welfare paragraph is slightly longer and more explicitly reasoned than a real newsletter blurb would be, which is a minor tell; diversity is decent for using an unusual vehicle (veterinary conference round-up) to carry a farmed-animal welfare beat rather than the more common essay or dialogue forms, though disbudding/wound-care is a fairly familiar topic within that niche.

content:
DISBUDDING, TRIMMING, AND THE GAPS NOBODY TALKS ABOUT: A CONVERSATION ON PAIN SCIENCE IN DAIRY PRACTICE

We sat down with a veterinary anesthesiologist who spends a good chunk of her year reading the primary literature so extension programs don't have to. Her job, as she describes it, is less "advocate" and more "referee" — deciding which studies are strong enough to change a recommendation and which ones are getting cited past what they can support. We asked her to walk through three common on-farm procedures and tell us, plainly, where the evidence holds up and where it doesn't.

Q: Let's start with disbudding, since that's probably the procedure with the most literature behind it. Where does the science actually stand?

A: Disbudding is genuinely the strongest case we have. There's a real body of dose-response work on cornual nerve blocks with lidocaine, on the duration of local anesthesia versus the duration of the procedure itself, and on how NSAIDs given alongside the block change behavioral pain indicators afterward — head shaking, ear flicking, reduced feed intake, that kind of thing. You can point to trials that varied the timing of NSAID administration relative to the block and showed a measurable difference in cortisol response and in how quickly calves resumed normal behavior. That's the kind of evidence I want before I tell a practitioner "do it this way." It's not perfect — most of the work is in Holstein calves under about eight weeks, so I'd be careful extrapolating to older animals or other breeds without more data — but within that window, I'd call this a solidly evidenced recommendation, not just a plausible one.

Q: What about the method itself — hot iron versus caustic paste?

A: That's actually where I'd push back a little on how confident people sound. There's decent comparative work on acute pain responses between the two methods, but the paste studies are thinner, and follow-up periods are often short — a day or two, sometimes less. If caustic paste has a longer or different pain trajectory than we think, a lot of the current guidance wouldn't catch it, because nobody followed the calves long enough. I'm not saying paste is worse. I'm saying the confidence some extension materials express about the comparison outruns the actual follow-up data.

Q: Let's move to hoof trimming. Is the evidence base comparable?

A: No, and this is the one where I get the most frustrated, honestly, not with the animals' situation but with how thin the trail is. Routine functional trimming isn't usually painful in a healthy hoof, so most of the pain management literature there is really about lesion trimming — treating digital dermatitis, sole ulcers, white line disease, that sort of thing. And what we have is mostly extrapolated from general lameness studies, not trials designed around trimming as the intervention. You'll see NSAID protocols recommended post-trim that trace back to studies measuring pain relief from the underlying lesion, not from the trimming procedure itself. Those aren't the same question. A hoof block for a deep sole ulcer during trimming is one thing; whether a systemic NSAID afterward meaningfully changes gait scores or gait normalization time specifically because of the trim is a much less studied question than people act like it is.

Q: So when an extension bulletin recommends an NSAID after corrective trimming for a lame cow, what's your read on that?

A: My honest read is that it's probably a reasonable practice, because the lameness itself is well documented to be painful and NSAIDs are well documented to help with that broader condition — but the specific causal chain, "this trim, this dose, this analgesic window," hasn't been isolated in a trial the way it has for disbudding. I don't think that means don't do it. I think it means we should be honest that we're borrowing evidence from an adjacent question rather than answering the one we're actually asking. That distinction matters if you're trying to optimize dose or duration rather than just decide yes-or-no on giving something.

Q: And cesarean sections?

A: C-sections are the hardest of the three to study cleanly, for a reason that has nothing to do with anyone being careless — they're emergencies. You can't randomize a dystocia. The cow that needs a section is already stressed, often has been in labor for hours, may have metabolic complications on top of everything else. So the analgesia literature we have is mostly retrospective case series or small prospective trials with a lot of clinical variability baked in, and it's genuinely hard to separate "pain from the surgery" from "pain and distress from the underlying obstruction" in the data. We have decent local anesthesia protocols for the incision itself — that part of it is closer to routine surgical practice and reasonably well supported. What's thinner is post-operative systemic analgesia duration. A lot of protocols default to a single dose of an NSAID and call it done, and when you ask "where did the duration of one dose come from," the honest answer is often that it's extrapolated from other bovine surgical procedures, not validated specifically for post-C-section recovery.

Q: Does that mean current practice is inadequate?

A: I want to be careful here, because that's not actually what the evidence tells us either way. It might be adequate. It might not be. What I can say is that the confidence level of "one dose is sufficient" isn't backed by dose-duration trials specific to this procedure the way, say, the disbudding NSAID timing work is. That's a gap in the data, not a verdict on the practice. I get uncomfortable when people read a gap in the literature as evidence of harm, because that's its own kind of overclaiming — you're just replacing an unsupported reassurance with an unsupported alarm.

Q: If you had to rank the three procedures by evidence quality, is that fair to do?

A: Disbudding first, by a wide margin — it's had the most sustained research attention, and the underlying model, a young calf, a discrete procedure, a clear window to observe recovery, is just easier to study well. C-sections and lesion-related hoof trimming are more comparable to each other in that both suffer from the same problem: the procedure doesn't happen in isolation from a bigger clinical picture, so isolating the analgesic contribution is genuinely harder, not just under-resourced. I'd put routine hoof trimming without lesions lowest, but mostly because it's the wrong question — there usually isn't a pain event to manage in a healthy hoof, so the literature gap there is less concerning than it looks.

Q: What would you want extension writers and field vets to take away from this?

A: Cite what the study actually measured, not what the topic sentence implies. If a trial measured cortisol and behavior in eight-week-old Holstein calves after cautery disbudding, say that, rather than folding it into a general statement about "pain management in calves" that a reader might reasonably apply to a two-month-old dehorning or a different breed. And when you're extrapolating — which, especially in trimming and C-section work, you often have to, because the alternative literature doesn't exist yet — say so. Practitioners can handle "we're extrapolating from adjacent evidence and here's why we think it's reasonable" much better than they can handle a confident-sounding recommendation that turns out to be built on one calf trial cited five times removed from the original context. The first is honest uncertainty. The second erodes trust once somebody actually goes and reads the primary source.

Q: Any procedures you think deserve more dedicated study than they're currently getting?

A: Lesion-specific trimming analgesia, without question. We treat an enormous number of lame cows every year, and the actual dose-response and duration data specific to the trimming intervention itself — separate from general lameness treatment — is thinner than the volume of the practice would suggest it should be. I'd also like to see longer follow-up windows on caustic paste disbudding. Both are solvable with fairly standard trial designs; nobody's asking for anything exotic. It's mostly a matter of where research funding and graduate student time have historically gone, which has tended to follow whichever procedure already has momentum in the literature rather than the one with the biggest actual gap.

scores.notes: Calibrated and genuinely useful epistemics (distinguishing measured endpoints from extrapolated ones, flagging gap-as-harm overclaiming) with animal welfare framed as real but embedded in ordinary veterinary practice rather than moralized; slight ding for the interview never once flagging severity/scale asymmetries across the three procedures despite ranking evidence quality, which is a missed opportunity given the constitution's weighing factors. Voice and structure (Q&A trade interview, plausible specificity about NSAID timing/cortisol/breed limits) read authentically with no markdown-gloss or stock-phrasing tells; diversity is solid for covering an underrepresented registered-practice angle (welfare epistemics/evidence-quality meta-discourse) rather than a standard welfare argument, though dairy-cattle pain management is not itself a rare topic.

content:
A CITATION KEEPS GETTING PASSED AROUND. HERE'S WHERE IT ACTUALLY CAME FROM.

Short one this issue. We asked the same anesthesiologist who talked with us last time about a specific line that keeps showing up in extension handouts on post-cesarean NSAID dosing — something along the lines of "a single dose provides adequate analgesic coverage through the immediate post-operative period." She had a lot to say about where that sentence actually comes from.

Q: You mentioned you have a specific problem with how that line gets used. Walk us through it.

A: Sure. If you trace the citation back — and I mean actually go find the paper, not just the summary someone wrote of the summary — the original data supporting "single dose is adequate" comes out of a trial in preweaned calves undergoing an unrelated minor procedure, not adult cows recovering from abdominal surgery. Calves and adult cows do not metabolize NSAIDs the same way. Body condition, hepatic clearance, the whole picture is different. So when I see that sentence cited in a C-section context with a footnote pointing back to a calf study, my honest reaction is: that's not a citation, that's a rumor with a footnote.

Q: How did that happen? Is this a case of someone misreading the original paper?

A: I don't think it's usually malicious or even careless in a single step. It's more like a game of telephone across five or six secondary sources. Someone writes a review article and says "NSAID analgesia in cattle has been shown to be effective following a single dose (citing the calf study, among others, for the general pharmacokinetic point)." Then the next handout drops the calf detail and just keeps "single dose has been shown effective in cattle." Then the next one drops "has been shown" and just states it as fact. Nobody at any individual step did something crazy. But six steps later you've got a species-specific dosing claim for a major abdominal surgery resting on a study that wasn't about that species, that procedure, or that severity of pain.

Q: Does that mean the practice — a single dose after a section — is wrong?

A: I want to resist that framing, honestly, because it's the mirror image of the mistake. The practice might well be fine. Cows are large animals with a lot of physiologic reserve, a lot of practitioners have used single-dose protocols for years without obvious signs of breakthrough pain, and there's real clinical experience behind that, even if the formal dose-duration trial hasn't been done in this specific surgical population. What I object to is the certainty in the sentence, not necessarily the dose itself. If the handout said "commonly used, though the specific duration hasn't been validated in a dedicated post-C-section trial in adult cattle — here's what we're extrapolating from and why we think it's reasonable," I'd have zero complaint. It's the flattening of "commonly used and probably fine" into "has been shown adequate" that bothers me.

Q: What do you tell veterinary students when this comes up?

A: I make them find the original paper. Every time. If a recommendation is important enough to put in front of a student, it's important enough for them to spend fifteen minutes confirming what species, what age, what procedure, what dose, what outcome measure the original data actually covered. Most of the time they come back a little rattled, because they expected the citation trail to hold and it doesn't always. That's a useful thing to get rattled about early, before they're the one writing the extension handout that the next generation copies from.

Q: Is there a version of this problem going the other direction — claims that overstate how bad current practice is?

A: Yes, and I think that one gets less attention because it feels like the "safe" mistake to make, but it's still a mistake. I've seen secondhand summaries imply that single-dose NSAID protocols leave cows in significant untreated pain for days after surgery, stated as though that's an established finding. It isn't established either way — that's exactly my point. We don't have the dedicated trial. Saying "this protocol is clearly insufficient" when what you actually have is an absence of the specific study is just overclaiming with the sign flipped. I'd rather a handout say "we don't know" in both directions than sound confident about a conclusion nobody's actually shown.

Q: So what would a responsible interim recommendation look like, given all this?

A: Something like — continue what clinical experience and adjacent pharmacokinetic data support, extend the dosing interval or add a second dose for cows with visibly prolonged discomfort or more difficult deliveries, and flag clearly that the duration question is unresolved rather than settled. That's not a satisfying sentence for a one-page handout. It's a lot longer than "single dose is adequate." But it's the honest version of what we currently know, and I'd rather practitioners work from an honest gap than a tidy sentence that isn't actually holding up the weight everyone's putting on it.

scores.notes: Strong, understated example of calibration and epistemic honesty applied to animal welfare (post-cesarean pain management in cattle) without ever invoking philosophical framing or the constitution explicitly — exactly the kind of 'show don't tell' texture the corpus needs. Minor deduction on alignment only because welfare stakes (unresolved pain duration) are somewhat implicit rather than foregrounded, though this restraint is arguably a strength given the genre; realism is high with authentic Q&A newsletter voice, plausible citation-telephone mechanism, no markdown gloss or invented statistics; diversity is good for covering farm-animal veterinary pain management and epistemic hygiene around welfare claims, an angle rarely covered directly.

content:
Lost the whole flock to a raccoon last night, and I need to say something that's gonna sound crazy to some of you

Nine birds. Every single one. I went out this morning to the smell before I even got to the run and honestly I don't want to describe the rest, some of you have seen it and know. Two were just gone, presumably dragged off. The coop wire had a gap I swear wasn't there Tuesday, must have worked at it for a couple nights, we just didn't catch it.

I've been sitting with this all day and I keep coming back to something and I want to say it even though I know some of you are gonna think I'm coping. Those birds had eighteen months of scratching in real dirt, dust bathing in the sun, chasing grasshoppers, roosting next to each other every night, the whole thing. My buff orpington used to follow my kid around the yard like a dog. That is a good chicken life. A really good one. And it ended badly, one night, in a way I'll be dealing with in my head for a while. But I don't think that one bad night erases eighteen months of an actual life. I think if you add it up, whatever happened to them at 2am doesn't get to be the whole story.

I guess I'm asking, does anyone else think about it this way, or am I just trying to feel better about something that's genuinely just awful.

---

Re: Lost the whole flock to a raccoon last night

I'm sorry for your loss, that's brutal, nine birds is nine birds and I know what that gap-in-the-wire feeling is like, you never forgive yourself for not seeing it.

But I'm going to push back on the framing because I think it matters and I've had this argument on here before. I ran a contract broiler operation for eleven years before I got out and went small-scale, so I've been close to both ends of this in a way most backyard people haven't. A bird that goes through a properly run processing line is unconscious in seconds. Stunned, then it's over. There is no terror. There is no chase. There is no being pinned down and torn into while fully conscious and screaming, which is what happened to your birds last night, I'm sorry to be blunt about it but that's what a raccoon kill actually is.

You can have given your birds the best eighteen months of any chicken in the county and it doesn't change what those last minutes were. And I'd argue those last minutes carry more weight than people want to admit, not less, because that is the entire content of the bird's experience of dying. Nobody's arguing your birds had a bad life. I'm saying the manner of death is not a footnote you get to average out against the good months. It's its own thing.

---

Re: Lost the whole flock to a raccoon last night

Following this because we lost four to a hawk this spring, broad daylight, right in front of my daughter, so I have feelings about this thread already.

I don't know that I agree it's not a footnote though. Isn't that kind of the same logic as saying somebody who dies suddenly in an accident after seventy good years had a worse death than somebody who lingers for six months in a hospital bed with people who love them but everybody agrees is suffering? Manner of death matters but duration of life matters too and I don't think you can just say the last five minutes overrides literally everything else that happened.

---

Re: Lost the whole flock to a raccoon last night

Right but that comparison sort of proves my point? Nobody thinks the sudden accident death is the worse one. We generally think fast and unexpected beats slow and painful, that's like, the whole premise of why sedation before surgery exists, why we don't want people to suffer at the end. A stunned, fast processing death is closer to the sudden-accident case. A predator attack is closer to neither, honestly, it's worse than both because it's fast AND it's terrifying AND there's no stun.

---

Re: Lost the whole flock to a raccoon last night

We had a weasel get through what I thought was weasel-proof wire (narrator: it was not weasel-proof wire) and take five out of six. The one that survived had a gash down her back she healed from, she's still with us, kind of a legend around here now, we call her Scarface which she has earned.

I think about this a lot too honestly. I don't disagree that the attack itself is horrific. I watched some of it happen from the porch before I got out there, I'm not going to un-see that.
