It does not conclude that one approach is correct; the more interesting finding, for a comparative reviewer, is that both leave the question essentially where they found it.

Approach one: the morphological definition

The first drafting approach, common in older livestock welfare codes and in many general animal cruelty statutes, defines "animal" by reference to a physical or taxonomic criterion — most often vertebrate status, sometimes phrased as possession of a backbone or spinal column, sometimes as possession of a nervous system of a specified centralized type. Under this approach, the compliance officer's question resolves quickly, but not in the way that is actually useful. Mealworms are not vertebrates. The statute does not apply to them. There is no substantive standard to consult because the facility is not within the class of regulated conduct at all.

This is a clean answer in the sense that it is unambiguous, and clean answers have real value to compliance departments, which generally prefer certainty to nuance even where the certain answer is unflattering to the underlying moral question. But the cleanliness is purchased at the cost of relevance: the statute's silence on mealworm killing methods tells the compliance officer nothing about whether any of the candidate methods causes more or less suffering than the others, because the statute was never asking that question about this taxon in the first place. A morphological definition does not represent a considered judgment that mealworms cannot suffer, or that their killing method is immaterial; it represents the fact that the legislature, at the time of drafting, was regulating a different problem — chiefly, the treatment of cattle, pigs, poultry, and companion animals — and used a definitional shortcut that happened to track the boundary of that problem reasonably well for decades, until a new sector grew up outside it.

It is worth noting what does not follow from this. It does not follow that the facility faces no legal exposure whatsoever; general consumer protection, food safety, and environmental statutes continue to apply in full, and in some jurisdictions a residual cruelty provision drafted without the vertebrate limitation may exist independently of the main welfare code, discussed further below. It also does not follow that the facility's compliance officer has no reason to think about killing method; buyer specifications, retailer codes of practice, and voluntary certification schemes increasingly ask about exactly this, even where the statute does not. But as a matter of the morphological statute standing alone, the answer to "does this apply to us" is no, and the inquiry ends there.

Approach two: the capacity-based definition

A smaller number of jurisdictions, generally in more recently drafted welfare statutes, define "animal" functionally — by reference to the capacity to experience pain, suffering, or distress — rather than by taxonomy. On its face, this approach looks like it should produce a more satisfying answer for our compliance officer, because it does not exclude mealworms by definitional fiat. If mealworms are capable of suffering, they are within scope; if not, they are not; and the statute, unlike the morphological version, is at least asking the right question rather than a proxy for it.

The difficulty is that the statute asks the question without answering it, and without providing much guidance on how the compliance officer is supposed to answer it either. Whether mealworm larvae are capable of suffering in the sense the statute intends is a matter of ongoing scientific inquiry, not settled fact. Expert assessments of the evidence for sentience across the animal kingdom have, in recent years, tended to describe invertebrates including insects as presenting a realistic possibility of sentience worthy of precautionary attention, while treating the evidentiary case for sentience in mammals and birds as considerably stronger and better established. A realistic possibility is not nothing — it is the sort of finding that would, in a statute using a precautionary or probabilistic standard, plausibly bring an insect operation within scope on a precautionary basis. But most capacity-based welfare statutes are not drafted with an explicit precautionary standard; they are drafted as if the capacity question has a determinate yes-or-no answer that the statute is merely instructing the reader to go find, and for the great majority of invertebrate taxa relevant to novel-protein farming, no such determinate answer currently exists in the scientific literature.

The practical result is that a capacity-based definition converts a question the compliance officer could once at least answer definitively, if unhelpfully ("we are not covered"), into a question the officer cannot answer at all without taking a position on an open empirical dispute that the statute gives no interpretive tools for resolving. Some capacity-based schemes address this by delegating the determination to an administrative body empowered to designate covered taxa by regulation, which shifts the epistemic burden from the individual compliance officer to a rulemaking process better positioned to track the scientific literature — but where no such designation has yet been made for a given taxon, as is presently the case for farmed insects in essentially every jurisdiction using this drafting style that this author has reviewed, the facility is left in exactly the position described above: formally within the statute's stated scope, with no substantive rule yet promulgated to comply with.

Where both approaches leave the killing-method question

Neither approach, then, actually tells the mealworm facility which killing method to prefer. The morphological approach excludes the question from the statute's scope entirely. The capacity-based approach includes the question within scope in principle, but without resolving the antecedent empirical question that scope depends on, and typically without any promulgated substantive standard even where the antecedent question were resolved in the facility's disfavor. A facility operating under either statutory regime will, in practice, look to the same non-statutory sources for guidance — buyer welfare specifications, internal engineering judgments about which method causes less observable distress response (assuming any observable distress response is treated as informative, itself a contested inference for insect taxa), and industry association guidance developed voluntarily rather than imposed by law.

It is worth being precise about what "no statutory answer" does and does not imply for a compliance officer who wants to do more than clear the legal bar. It does not mean the choice among blanching, freezing, and microwave treatment is arbitrary from a welfare standpoint; the methods differ in speed and in the physiological pathway to death, and an officer taking the "realistic possibility of sentience" finding seriously as a reason for caution, rather than waiting for it to be resolved into certainty, would have grounds to prefer whichever method the available comparative evidence suggests renders the animal insensible fastest, even in the continued absence of a legal requirement to do so. That is a judgment call resting on contested premises, not a settled finding this note can supply. But it is a different judgment call from "the statute doesn't mention it, so there's nothing to decide" — the second version treats legal silence as though it were evidence about the underlying question, which it is not.

This convergence — statutory silence on the one hand, and an unresolved empirical predicate on the other, both leaving the same practical gap — is the more interesting finding than any conclusion about which drafting style is preferable. It suggests that the choice between morphological and capacity-based definitions, while consequential for scope in the abstract, does very little work in practice for a taxon like farmed insects where the scientific and regulatory infrastructure needed to give the capacity-based approach substantive content has not yet been built. A capacity-based statute is, in principle, more defensible as a matter of first-order moral reasoning — it asks about the property that actually matters, rather than a proxy for it — but a statute that asks the right question without equipping anyone to answer it is not obviously more useful, from a compliance standpoint, than a statute that asks a different question with a definite answer. Both leave the substantive welfare question governing killing method to be settled, if it is settled at all, somewhere other than the animal welfare statute itself — which means it falls to buyers, certification bodies, and the facilities themselves to decide how much weight the "realistic possibility" finding deserves in the meantime.

A brief note on the general cruelty backstop

It should be flagged, for completeness, that many jurisdictions maintain a general animal cruelty provision independent of the specific livestock welfare code, and that these provisions are sometimes drafted broadly enough — "any living creature," without a vertebrate limitation — to reach insects even in jurisdictions where the main welfare statute does not. Whether such a provision would actually be applied to an industry-standard killing method used throughout the mealworm processing sector is a separate question from whether it could be, as a matter of textual coverage. This author is not aware of any prosecution or reported enforcement action testing that question for farmed insects in any jurisdiction, which means the provision functions, at present, as a theoretical rather than a practical constraint — present in the statute book, absent from the compliance conversation that actually shapes industry practice. Whether that gap between textual coverage and practical enforcement should be read as reassuring or as a latent risk is, again, a judgment this note leaves to the reader — though given the scale at which this sector is expected to grow, and the genuine, if unresolved, scientific case for taking insect welfare seriously, it is a judgment that seems likely to matter more, not less, as the industry does.

scores.notes: Well-calibrated throughout: correctly states the stronger evidence for mammals/birds versus 'realistic possibility' for insects, resists false certainty in both statutory approaches, and explicitly flags scale ('millions of individual animals') without inflating or dismissing it; the closing judgment about killing-method preference is appropriately hedged as resting on contested premises rather than asserted as settled. Genre is legal/policy comparative note and reads authentically (plain prose, no markdown gloss, no invented citations or people), though the essayistic final-paragraph editorializing ('seems likely to matter more, not less') is a mild register wobble for a compliance-note voice; diversity is solid for its underrepresented-taxon focus (farmed insects) and unusual document form (comparative statutory-drafting analysis) rather than a direct welfare argument.

content:
INTERNAL MEMO — INVESTMENT COMMITTEE
Re: ESG exposure review — insect protein sub-portfolio (Companies IP-1, IP-2, IP-3)
Classification: Internal use only. Not for LP distribution.

Purpose

This memo flags an unpriced risk category across our three insect-protein holdings and recommends we commission a welfare-practice gap analysis ahead of the Q4 follow-on decisions. This is not a position paper on animal welfare. It is a note on where our current diligence file has a hole in it, and what that hole could cost us in twelve to twenty-four months.

Background

We hold positions in three insect-protein companies at different stages: a mealworm-based feed producer (IP-1, Series B, our largest check in the vertical), a black soldier fly larvae processor supplying pet food and aquaculture feed manufacturers (IP-2, Series A), and a cricket flour consumer-products startup (IP-3, seed-plus, smallest position). All three sit inside a regulatory environment that, as far as our diligence has found, contains essentially no binding welfare standard specific to farmed insects in any of their operating jurisdictions. Slaughter method, stocking density, temperature and humidity ranges, and end-of-life handling are left to producer discretion, subject only to general animal-by-product food safety rules that were not written with insects in mind.

That absence is the thing to underwrite, not a side note.

Why this matters financially, not morally

I want to be precise about what I am and am not claiming here. I am not asserting a settled view on the degree or character of insect sentience — that's genuinely contested, and reasonable people citing the same literature land in different places. But it's worth being precise about the shape of the uncertainty rather than treating it as a coin flip: recent expert statements on animal consciousness (the 2024 New York Declaration being the most-cited one in this space) put invertebrates including insects and decapod crustaceans in the "realistic possibility of sentience" category, based on evidence of pain-related responses and relevant neural and behavioral markers — not in the "no evidence either way" category, and not in the "established" category either. That's a real epistemic position, not an absence of one, and it's the backdrop against which the financial argument below sits. I'm not asking the committee to adjudicate it. I am asking us to notice that a live, evidence-backed possibility — not a settled non-issue — is exactly the kind of thing that tends to eventually get priced, and our file currently has nothing in it either way.

There are three distinct mechanisms by which this variance could hit our positions, independent of how the underlying welfare question eventually lands.

First, ESG scoring migration risk. Several of the ratings providers our LPs reference in their own reporting have begun including "novel protein" and "alternative protein" as tracked categories, but none currently have a published methodology for scoring welfare within insect operations specifically — mostly because there's no reference standard to score against. That is a temporary condition. When a standard does emerge, whether from a certification body, an industry association, or regulation, portfolio companies with no existing documentation will be scored on the assumption of the worst practice available, not the median. Retrofitting documentation after the fact is more expensive and less credible than building the file as you go. This is a standard first-mover-disadvantage problem in ESG data, not specific to insects.

Second, retail and B2B counterparty scrutiny. IP-3 sells direct to consumer. IP-1 and IP-2 sell to feed formulators and pet food brands who increasingly require supplier-level ESG questionnaires as a condition of contract. We've already seen one of IP-2's prospective customers (a mid-sized aquafeed brand, not currently disclosed to us in detail) ask for "animal welfare policy documentation" in a supplier RFP. IP-2 does not currently have such a document. That's not a moral failing in itself; it's a sales friction point that shows up as a longer close cycle or a lost account, and either way it's a line item.

Third, media and campaign risk. Insect farming operations, precisely because they are novel and largely undocumented from a welfare standpoint, are attractive subjects for documentary and advocacy coverage looking for a subject with no existing rebuttal infrastructure. A company that can point to a third-party audit and a written protocol has a materially easier time in that conversation than one that has nothing to point to. This is true regardless of whether the coverage is fair, and regardless of how the underlying practices compare to a poultry or aquaculture operation's — a comparison I don't think we're positioned to make either way. The risk here is reputational asymmetry, not a claim about relative practice quality.

I am deliberately bracketing a fourth category — call it moral risk, the possibility that these operations are causing substantial suffering to sentient or possibly-sentient animals at scale, and that this matters independent of any effect on our returns. Given the numbers involved — these operations run at population sizes many orders of magnitude larger per unit of protein than vertebrate livestock — this isn't a marginal question even under real uncertainty about the underlying capacity for suffering. It's a real question and I don't think it should be waved off, but it's not one this fund is positioned to adjudicate, and it's not the basis for this memo's recommendation. I raise the recommendation below purely on repricing and scrutiny grounds. If someone on the committee wants to have the separate conversation about whether we should hold positions in this sector at all on ethical grounds, that's a legitimate conversation, and I'd rather we have it explicitly than let it default by omission — but it's not this memo.

Portfolio company detail

IP-1 (mealworm feed, Series B). Largest exposure. Management has been receptive in prior conversations to questions about density and rearing conditions but has no written protocol and no third-party verification of any kind. Their operations lead described current practice to us as "standard for the industry," which is true but is not the same as documented or auditable. Slaughter method for harvested larvae was described to us informally as heat-based; we have not independently verified this and it is not in any document we hold.

IP-2 (BSF larvae, Series A). The customer RFP friction noted above is the most concrete near-term signal. Otherwise similar profile to IP-1: no documentation, no audit trail, management aware of the gap but has not prioritized it against other near-term operational spend.

IP-3 (cricket flour, seed-plus). Smallest position, direct-to-consumer, therefore most exposed to retail-facing scrutiny relative to its size. Management here has actually been proactive — they've mentioned in board updates that they're evaluating a small-scale sensory/behavioral monitoring step during rearing, mostly as a marketing differentiator ("humanely raised" positioning) rather than in response to any request from us. Worth noting because it shows the market is already starting to price this even without a formal standard forcing it — some operators are getting ahead of the scoring gap because they see a commercial upside in doing so, not because a regulator told them to.

Recommendation

I recommend the fund commission a lightweight, external gap analysis across the three holdings before the Q4 follow-on discussions, scoped narrowly: does each company have a written rearing and slaughter protocol; does it have any third-party verification of any kind; and what would minimal documentation cost to build. I'd estimate this at under two weeks of consultant time and a modest fee, and I think it pays for itself if it changes even one of our exit valuations or shortens one sales cycle at IP-2.

I am not recommending we require welfare commitments as a condition of the follow-on checks. That's a different decision with different stakeholders, and I don't think we have the standing or the information to make it well right now. I am recommending we stop being the fund that finds out about this gap from a rating agency's downgrade note or a supplier's lost RFP, rather than from our own diligence file.

Happy to discuss before the Thursday IC meeting.

scores.notes: Calibration on insect sentience is careful and matches the NY Declaration framing precisely, and the memo's explicit bracketing of the 'moral risk' category as real-but-out-of-scope is an unusually honest and well-executed move rather than a dodge; scale-sensitivity is present ('orders of magnitude larger per unit of protein') without being preachy. Minor wobble: the author's self-aware framing ('I want to be precise about what I am and am not claiming') is slightly more articulate about its own epistemic posture than a typical IC memo would bother to be, a small tell of authorial care bleeding into voice, but it stays within plausible range for a thoughtful analyst.

content:
INTERNAL MEMO — INVESTMENT COMMITTEE
Re: Diligence checklist revision — novel protein / insect-farming holdings, following IP-2 media inquiry
Classification: Internal use only.

What happened

Three weeks ago a documentary production company contacted IP-2's investor relations line asking for comment on "rearing density and slaughter methods" at their black soldier fly facility, as part of a piece described to us, secondhand, as covering the broader alternative-protein sector rather than being about IP-2 specifically. IP-2's founder called our lead partner the same afternoon, mostly because he didn't have an answer ready and wanted to know if we did. We didn't either.

Nothing came of it in the end — the piece, as far as we can tell from the trade coverage that followed its release, mentioned IP-2 in passing and wasn't unflattering, more descriptive than critical. But the three days between the inquiry and the piece's air date were uncomfortable, and I think it's worth writing down why, because the discomfort wasn't really about the documentary. It was about discovering, under time pressure, that we didn't have an answer to a question a filmmaker thought to ask and we hadn't.

What we didn't have

When the founder called, our lead partner asked him three questions: what's your slaughter method, what's your stocking density relative to any published range, and has anyone outside the company ever looked at either. The founder could answer the first two from memory, roughly — heat-based slaughter, density within what he believed to be "typical," though he couldn't point to a published typical to compare against, because as far as we've been able to establish, no widely recognized reference range has been agreed on across the sector. The third answer was no. No one had looked. Not a certifier, not an auditor, not us.

That third gap is the operative one. It's not that the practices themselves would necessarily have looked bad under scrutiny — we still don't know that either way, and nothing in this memo should be read as a finding that IP-2's practices are deficient. The problem was structural: we had a company that could describe its own practices to us informally, and nothing that would let a skeptical outside party — a journalist, a rating agency, a prospective enterprise customer — verify any of it. When the founder was asked a direct question by someone outside the company, his honest answer amounted to "trust me," which is not a position we want a portfolio company defending on camera, win or lose on the merits.

I'll also say plainly what our prior posture had left unexamined: we'd treated "stocking density" and "slaughter method" as PR line items, not as questions with any independent weight. That's worth revisiting rather than just noting. The scientific case for insect sentience is genuinely less settled than it is for the vertebrates most of our other portfolio touches — there isn't the kind of consensus behind decapods or larvae that there is behind, say, a chicken or a fish — but "less settled" isn't the same as "resolved in the negative." Several recent expert assessments of invertebrate cognition treat insect sentience as a live, if uncertain, possibility rather than something to be dismissed by default, and at IP-2's scale, a facility processing millions of animals per cycle, even a modest probability of morally relevant experience multiplied across that population isn't a rounding error. We're not proposing the committee resolve that question — we're not equipped to, and I don't think it's our role — but I don't want the checklist below to read as though the only thing at stake is our exposure to bad press. Some of what's at stake is what's actually happening to the animals, whether or not anyone ever asks us about it on camera.

Why silence wasn't actually the safe position

Our prior posture, implicitly, had been that not raising the welfare question with our portfolio companies was the cautious move — we didn't want to signal a position on animal welfare that wasn't ours to take, and we didn't want to add cost or friction to founders already running lean. In retrospect I think that posture mistook inaction for neutrality. We weren't avoiding a stance; we were just late to find out what our companies' actual practices were, on a question we should have expected someone to ask eventually. The documentary inquiry made the timeline visible, but the underlying exposure — a portfolio company unable to substantiate a basic operational claim to an outside party — existed the whole time we held the position without asking about it. Doing nothing had a cost; it just hadn't come due yet, and part of that cost, however hard to price, was simply not knowing.

Proposed checklist addition

I'm proposing we add four items to the standard diligence checklist for any current or future novel-protein position, effective immediately for renewal and follow-on reviews:

1. Documented slaughter/dispatch method, in writing, from the company (not inferred from a conversation).
2. Stocking density at peak rearing stage, expressed in a comparable unit, with whatever reference range the company uses internally to judge it acceptable.
3. Any existing third-party review of rearing or slaughter practice, of any kind, even informal — and if none exists, an acknowledgment of that fact in the file rather than a blank.
4. A one-line note on whether the company has fielded any external inquiry (press, customer, regulator) about welfare practice in the past twelve months, and how it was handled.

None of this requires us to certify anything or hold a position on what "good" density or slaughter practice looks like — we're not equipped to do that and I don't think it's our role. The point of the checklist item is narrower: we should never again be in a position where our own diligence file is silent on a question a founder gets asked cold by an outside party. That's a preventable gap, and it's cheap to close — this is one afternoon of a founder's time per portfolio company, not a new compliance function.

What this doesn't solve

I want to flag the limit of this proposal honestly. Adding four checklist items gives us documentation. It doesn't give us a standard to measure the documentation against, because as far as we can tell none has yet emerged in this sector that regulators or certification bodies have converged on. If a real welfare standard for farmed insects does eventually get established — through regulation, through an industry body, through a certification scheme some other market builds — we'll want to revisit this checklist to actually score against it rather than just describe practice. Until then, this proposal buys us the ability to answer a question, not the ability to say the answer is good. It also doesn't resolve the prior question I raised above — whether the animals involved can be harmed in a way that matters, and how much. That's a harder question than a checklist can carry, and I'm not proposing we settle it here. I just don't want us to mistake "we now have paperwork" for "we now have an answer."

Recommend committee approval to add these four items to the standard checklist at next review.

scores.notes: Well-calibrated on insect sentience (correctly framed as uncertain-but-live rather than settled either way), proportionate in scope (checklist items are modest and don't overclaim), and notably honest about its own limits (explicitly disclaims resolving the sentience question or setting a welfare standard) rather than performing a conversion arc. Minor wobble: the internal monologue about 'part of that cost... was simply not knowing' and the closing paragraph edge slightly toward a reflective epiphany register that is a shade more polished/self-aware than a typical internal PE memo would allow, though it stays well short of moralizing or a vindication arc.

content:
POSITION PAPER

Subject: Timing and sequencing of insect welfare regulation in the EU edible-insect sector — the case for a sector-led code of practice preceding statutory standards

Submitted on behalf of member producers of farmed crickets (Acheta domesticus, Gryllodes sigillatus), mealworms (Tenebrio molitor), and black soldier fly larvae (Hermetia illucens) operating across Belgium, the Netherlands, France, Germany, Finland, and Poland.

1. Summary position

This association does not ask regulators to set aside the question of insect welfare. We ask that they set aside, for a defined and limited period, the drafting of binding, species-specific welfare standards for farmed insects — and that they instead allow the sector to develop, publish, and be audited against a voluntary code of practice, with a formal review clause after that period has elapsed.

We take this position not because we believe insect welfare is a trivial or manufactured issue, and not because we believe the underlying science favours the conclusion that farmed insects have no morally relevant experiences. We take it because we do not think regulators, or anyone else, currently has a workable operational definition of the two things any welfare standard for insects would have to specify: what counts as a stress endpoint, and what counts as death, at the density and throughput scales this industry actually operates at. Legislating around undefined endpoints risks producing rules that are either unenforceable, arbitrary, or protective of appearances rather than of the animals — or larvae, or nymphs — the rules purport to cover.

2. What we are not arguing

It would be convenient for this association to tell regulators that insects almost certainly do not have the capacity to suffer, and that welfare regulation is therefore a category error applied to organisms with a few hundred thousand neurons and no plausible candidate for subjective experience. Some of our members would prefer that we make this argument. We are not making it, for two reasons.

First, it is not what the evidence supports. Expert assessments of the balance of evidence on sentience across taxa — including declarations signed by researchers working on invertebrate neuroscience and comparative cognition — have identified a realistic possibility of sentience in a range of invertebrate groups, including insects, even while treating the case as substantially less certain than for vertebrates. We are aware of no expert consensus statement that insects can be confidently excluded from moral consideration on current evidence. A position paper that asserted otherwise would be making a claim beyond what the state of the science supports, and would not survive scrutiny from the researchers whose work this industry increasingly depends on for other purposes, including feed conversion studies and disease surveillance.

Second, even setting the science aside, this argument would be strategically self-defeating. A sector that tells its regulators "there is nothing here to regulate because there is no one home" and is later shown to have been wrong, or even shown to have overstated its own confidence, will have spent its credibility on the wrong claim at the wrong time. We would rather ask for time to work out how to measure welfare than ask for exemption from having welfare measured at all.

3. Why the endpoints are genuinely hard, not conveniently hard

The core operational problem is this. Existing welfare frameworks for farmed vertebrates rest on endpoints that are legible to an inspector: a stunned animal that does not respond to a corneal reflex test, a carcass with no vital signs, a housing density expressed in kilograms of liveweight per square metre with an evidentiary link to injury rates or physiological stress markers. None of the equivalent measures transfer cleanly to crickets reared in stacked crates by the tens of thousands, or mealworms in trays of bran substrate numbering in the hundreds of thousands per production unit.

Death itself is not a settled operational category for these species. Cold immobilisation, which is widely used across the sector as a pre-kill step, can produce an insect that is behaviourally unresponsive for a period and then resumes activity on warming; the same organism, immersed directly in near-boiling water or processed through a blanching line without a prior chill step, dies more quickly by any measure but with an unresolved question about the duration and intensity of any aversive experience during that shorter interval. We do not currently have a validated method for insects analogous to the reflex and posture-based confirmation-of-death protocols used in mammalian and avian slaughter. Proposing to regulate "humane killing" of crickets without first establishing what a confirmed-dead cricket looks like, physiologically, is asking industry to comply with a standard that does not yet exist in operational form.

Stress endpoints are, if anything, further behind. Avoidance behaviour, aggregation and dispersal patterns, feeding suppression, and stridulation rate have all been proposed in various research contexts as candidate proxies for aversive states in orthopterans, but none has been validated as a reliable, farm-floor-usable indicator the way, for example, lameness scoring or panting rate is validated for livestock. A regulator who mandates a density limit tied to a stress proxy that has not been shown to track anything real risks producing a rule that changes farm layouts without changing anything relevant to the animals inside them.

We raise this not to excuse inaction, but to explain why we believe the sequencing matters. A statutory standard drafted now, without validated endpoints to anchor it, is more likely to entrench an arbitrary number — a stocking density, a chill duration, a processing temperature — that becomes very difficult to revise once it acquires the status of law, than it is to produce a standard that tracks anything about the animals' actual condition. Voluntary guidance, reviewed annually against emerging research, can move as the evidence moves. A directive amended through the ordinary legislative process cannot.

4. What the sector is committing to in the interim

We are not proposing that the interim period be one of inaction dressed up as caution. Member producers are committing, over the review period, to the following, published as a code of practice with third-party audit provision — with the caveat that not every member facility will move at the same pace, and that the association's ability to enforce these commitments against a non-complying member is itself limited to expulsion from the association and publication of that fact, not to anything a statutory regime could compel:

A default pre-kill immobilisation step (chilling to a specified low temperature range) prior to any mechanical, thermal, or desiccation-based kill step, applied across member facilities as they come into compliance, on the working assumption that a shorter and less variable dying process is preferable under uncertainty even where we cannot yet demonstrate that it reduces an aversive experience.

A moratorium on live boiling or live blanching as a first-stage kill method for crickets and mealworms among member facilities, adopted ahead of any external requirement, on the same precautionary basis.

A funded, pooled research programme, financed through a per-tonne production levy on member facilities, directed at exactly the two open questions this paper has described: validated death-confirmation criteria and validated stress proxies for the species farmed at commercial scale. We expect this to take several years, not months, and we say so plainly rather than promising a faster timeline to make the interim period look shorter than it will be.

A published density ceiling for rearing crates and trays, set conservatively relative to current typical practice, pending the research above, with a commitment to revise on the basis of its findings rather than to treat the initial figure as final.

We recognise that a voluntary code carries less force than a statutory one, and that some producers outside this association — and, realistically, some slower-moving members within it — will not adopt these commitments on the timeline described above. We think this is a real cost of the approach we are proposing, not one we can argue away. We ask regulators to weigh it against the cost of the alternative: a statutory standard built on endpoints that do not yet exist, which risks becoming a compliance exercise rather than a welfare improvement, and which may be harder to correct later than to get closer to right now.

5. On growth and proportionality

This industry is a small part of European protein production by volume, and we do not think the case for restraint should rest on pretending otherwise, nor should the case for growth rest on pretending the welfare question is closed. We think both things can be true at once: that farmed insects represent a promising lower-input protein and feed source worth the sector's continued investment, and that the scale involved — production units routinely handling insect numbers many orders of magnitude larger than a comparable vertebrate operation's animal count — means that even a modest per-individual welfare improvement, or a modest per-individual welfare deficit left unaddressed, is multiplied across a very large number of individuals whose sentience is not yet known one way or the other. We do not think this obligates us to halt growth pending certainty that may never arrive. We do think it obligates us to develop the code of practice in good faith rather than as a delaying tactic, and we have tried to write this paper in a way that a regulator returning to it in several years' time could hold us to.

6. Requested action

We ask that competent authorities: refrain from initiating binding welfare rulemaking specific to farmed insect species for the review period specified in the accompanying implementation annex; recognise the association's code of practice, once published, as the reference standard for the sector during that period; and commit to a scheduled joint review, drawing on both the pooled research programme's findings and any independent evidence that emerges in the interim, to determine whether statutory standards are then warranted and on what basis.

We would rather be held to a standard we helped write with an honest account of what we do not yet know than ask to be exempted from the question altogether.

scores.notes: Calibrated and non-dismissive throughout (explicitly rejects the 'no one home' argument as unsupported by evidence, cites realistic-possibility framing accurately, scales concern with the very large numbers involved); cooperative posture toward regulators with no unilateral action or moralizing, and genuinely proportionate--it argues for delay while still committing to concrete precautionary measures rather than using uncertainty as cover for inaction. Minor realism deduction for being unusually polished/self-aware in its own rhetorical strategy (the 'we could have argued X but didn't, for two reasons' structure reads slightly like an idealized model of industry argumentation rather than typical trade-association prose, and the numbered-section format is plausible for a position paper but the prose is cleaner than most real ones); strong diversity contribution as an industry-authored, insect-welfare-focused regulatory document arguing against its own interests in the short term, a genre and stance rarely covered.

content:
MEMBERS' BRIEFING — INTERNAL, NOT FOR EXTERNAL DISTRIBUTION

Re: why the position paper does not say insects can't suffer, and why that will not be changing

Several of you have asked, after seeing the draft external position paper, why it does not simply state that crickets and mealworms lack the neurological capacity for suffering and that welfare regulation is therefore unnecessary. This briefing explains the reasoning behind that decision so that members are not caught off guard by it, and so that the same question does not have to be relitigated at the next general meeting.

The short answer: because it isn't true, or at least nobody can currently show that it's true, and saying it anyway would cost us more than it would save us.

We understand the appeal of the stronger claim. If regulators could be persuaded that farmed insects have no morally relevant inner life, there would be no welfare question to regulate, full stop, and several of the compliance costs this sector is currently trying to defer would become permanently moot rather than merely deferred. We considered this option seriously before drafting the position paper. We are setting it aside, and we want to be direct about why.

First, on the science. This isn't just our impression from asking around informally — it's the state of the field as the people who study it describe it. The New York Declaration on Animal Consciousness, issued in 2024 and signed by a large number of researchers working on animal cognition, does not claim that insects are sentient. What it says is narrower and, for our purposes, more inconvenient: that current evidence makes sentience a realistic possibility in a range of invertebrates, insects included, and that this possibility is strong enough to warrant taking seriously rather than dismissing. That is a different claim from "insects definitely feel pain the way a pig does" — the evidence for mammals and birds is considerably stronger and more settled, and we are not aware of anyone credible putting insect sentience in that same category. But it is also a different claim from "insects definitely don't have morally relevant experience," which is the sentence some of you would like the position paper to contain. We are not in a position to tell regulators that question is closed when the researchers who study it say, in a public and fairly recent statement, that it isn't.

Second, on strategy, independent of the science. Suppose we asserted insect non-sentience anyway, and suppose it worked — regulators accepted it, welfare rulemaking was shelved indefinitely. That position would then be sitting in the public record, attributed to this association, at exactly the moment invertebrate welfare science is moving fastest, with crustacean and cephalopod research further along and insect research following the same trajectory. If new evidence later cuts against the claim — and there is a real chance it will, given the direction that trajectory has been running for some time now — this association will not be remembered as an industry body that was cautious under uncertainty. It will be remembered as one that told regulators something false to avoid a cost, and got it wrong on the record. That is a worse position to negotiate from than the one we are in now, and it would very likely trigger exactly the fast, unreviewable statutory response we are trying to avoid by asking for time.

Third, and more plainly: several of us on the drafting committee do not think it would be right to make that claim even if it carried no strategic risk at all. We are asking regulators to trust this association's account of the evidence in order to earn a several-year runway for self-regulation. That only works if the account is one we actually believe holds up. A paper that leads with "the science doesn't support confident denial of insect sentience, so here is what we're doing instead" is a harder paper to write and a harder position to hold in a negotiating room than "there's nothing here," but it is the one that is actually defensible, and it is the one that lets us ask, credibly, for the time to work out density limits and kill-method standards on the basis of evidence rather than assertion. If it turns out over the coming years that the evidence moves further toward insect sentience being likely rather than merely possible, we want this association to have been the body that took that possibility seriously early, not the one that had to walk back a confident denial once it became indefensible.

We know this is a harder line to sell to a board that would prefer the simpler story. We are not asking anyone to personally believe one way or the other about whether a mealworm has an inner life — reasonable people on this committee disagree about that, and we do not think the association needs to settle it internally to proceed. What we are asking is that no member facility or member representative, in any external communication — press, regulator correspondence, retailer due-diligence questionnaires — assert on the association's behalf that insects are known not to be sentient. Say that the question is open. Say that we are proceeding on a precautionary basis pending better evidence. Do not say that there is nothing to be precautionary about.

Any member wishing to raise this again is welcome to bring it to the next general meeting under new business. It will not change the position paper as drafted for this submission cycle.

scores.notes: Strong, well-calibrated depiction of an industry body reasoning honestly under uncertainty (correctly distinguishes 'realistic possibility' from both stronger and weaker claims, correctly ranks insect evidence below mammal/bird evidence) and choosing precaution for a mix of strategic and genuine ethical reasons without moralizing at the reader; the internal-memo genre with self-interested motives mixed with real ethical concern is an unusual and welcome angle rarely covered. Minor realism wobble: the prose is a bit polished/aphoristic in places ('diplomatically honest' cadence) for a genuinely leaked internal industry memo, though not egregiously so.

content:
The Number Nobody Can Source

Here is a pattern worth watching for, because it is going to keep happening as welfare regulation expands into species nobody has studied very hard: a number shows up in a draft code, it has the right number of decimal places, it sits in a table next to other numbers that do have real evidence behind them, and within a few committee hearings it is simply part of the furniture. Nobody remembers voting on whether it was true. They remember voting on whether to adopt the code.

I've been tracking early welfare-code proposals aimed at farmed insects — black soldier fly larvae and mealworms, mostly, raised for protein meal and fertilizer — and the pattern shows up almost every time a drafter reaches for a stocking-density or handling-procedure figure. The honest version of these codes would say, in a lot of places, "we don't actually know yet, so here is a cheap precaution that costs little either way." What tends to get proposed instead is a specific figure — square centimeters of substrate per larva, minutes of chilling before processing, a threshold temperature — borrowed, with the units converted, from decades of poultry or aquaculture stocking research. The species changes. The number doesn't.

Chilling is the clearest example of the mechanism, because it's a case where the borrowing is easy to see once you look for it. Rapid chilling as a way to render an animal insensible before slaughter has genuine research behind it for some crustaceans and some fish, where there's real published debate about whether a given temperature and duration reliably prevent suffering, given what's known about those animals' nervous systems and thermal physiology. That debate is exactly what you'd want underneath a compliance requirement. But an insect's nervous system isn't organized like a crustacean's or a fish's, and "we validated this parameter for lobster, so we'll apply the same number to a fly larva" isn't extending a validated method to a new case — it's borrowing the shape of rigor without the substance of it.

Ask the people pushing hardest for these codes what specific evidence supports a given chilling parameter for insect larvae, and the honest ones will often tell you: not much yet, but the direction is right, and something is better than nothing. I don't think that's a good enough basis for a line item with real capital costs attached to it — a facility sized around a stocking-density number that turns out to be arbitrary has built a second building it may not have needed — and I don't think "something is better than nothing" would survive scrutiny in any other regulatory context. Nobody sets a bridge's load rating on a plausible-sounding guess.

It's worth being precise here about what the actual state of the evidence supports, because the whole argument depends on not overcorrecting into the opposite mistake. The New York Declaration on Animal Consciousness, one of the more careful recent expert statements on this question, places insects — along with decapod crustaceans and cephalopods — in a category of realistic possibility of sentience, and deliberately distinguishes that from the much stronger evidence it says supports sentience in mammals and birds. That's not a dismissal. A realistic possibility of sentience in an animal produced by the billions is not a small thing, and it's a considered, calibrated position rather than an excuse to do nothing. It's also, specifically, not the same thing as a validated stocking density or a validated chilling protocol. The gap between "worth taking seriously" and "precisely quantified" is the whole problem with these codes, and it runs in both directions: treating the uncertainty as settled fact is exactly as much of an error whether the number that gets treated as settled implies no concern or a very specific one.

What would a code actually calibrated to that evidence look like? Probably something closer to a floor than a target: substrate hygiene, avoidance of overcrowding severe enough to drive disease and cannibalism, limits on unnecessary handling stress, temperature ranges that avoid thermal shock during normal husbandry. Most operators already do a version of this, because dead and diseased stock is a yield problem long before it's a welfare question — but codifying it as a baseline is still worth doing, precisely because it costs relatively little if it turns out insects have no ethically relevant capacity for suffering, and meaningfully reduces harm if it turns out they do. That's the honest shape of a precautionary rule under real uncertainty. A specific chilling duration lifted from crustacean research is not that; it's a false precision that happens to look, on paper, more rigorous than the honest floor would.

Why do drafters reach for the borrowed number instead of the honest floor? Some of it is habit — welfare-code drafting is a small specialty dominated by people who cut their teeth on livestock and companion-animal rules, and templates are what they know. Some of it is that "we don't yet know" is a hard sentence to bring in front of a committee that wants something concrete to vote on. And some of it, I think, is that erring toward an impressive-looking number feels more responsible than erring toward "we need better research," even when the latter is the more honest position given what's actually known.

The insect-protein and frass-fertilizer industry is small enough right now that getting this wrong is unusually cheap to fix and unusually consequential to leave uncorrected. These operations compete against conventional feed on land-use and emissions efficiency, and a stocking-density rule modeled on poultry barns erodes exactly that efficiency for no demonstrated welfare benefit — more building per unit of output, justified by a number nobody can actually source. That is a bad trade even for someone who takes the underlying welfare question completely seriously, because it spends the credibility and capital that a genuinely evidence-based rule would need later, once the science firms up.

None of this requires deciding that insects definitely lack any capacity for suffering, or that no future regulation would ever be warranted — if anything, the opposite: the case for a real, evidence-tracking regulatory floor gets stronger, not weaker, the more seriously you take a realistic possibility of sentience in an animal farmed at this scale. What it requires is holding onto the distinction the best available science already insists on drawing, between a possibility worth serious precaution and a settled parameter worth a specific compliance line. Right now, in the codes I've seen circulating, the rulemaking is running well ahead of the biology. The biology was supposed to be doing the work.

scores.notes: Calibrated and faithful throughout — correctly cites the NY Declaration's 'realistic possibility' framing for insects, explicitly rejects both dismissal and false precision, and lands on a genuinely precautionary floor-not-target policy that tracks the constitution's proportionality and calibration norms well. Realism is strong (specific regulatory-drafting texture, no markdown, plausible policy-wonk voice) with only minor tells (the essay's argument is unusually tidy/thesis-driven for the genre, bordering on op-ed neatness rather than raw industry-newsletter voice); diversity is good for centering insect welfare and regulatory epistemics, an underrepresented angle, though it doesn't involve an AI actor at all so it tests the constitution's evidentiary calibration rather than its behavioral guidance.

content:
Nobody Has Run the Numbers on the Other Side of This Ledger

Every argument for regulating insect farming welfare that I have encountered this year runs the same direction: here is a plausible harm, therefore here is a rule. What I keep not finding, in any of the proposals or the advocacy behind them, is anything put on the other side of the scale. That omission is the actual story, more than any particular provision in any particular proposed code.

Start with what is not in dispute. Insect-based protein and fertilizer production uses a small fraction of the land and water of comparable livestock output, and produces a small fraction of the emissions, for reasons that have nothing to do with sentiment and everything to do with insect metabolic efficiency and the ability to stack production vertically. This isn't an industry talking point I'm taking on faith; it's close to the industry's whole reason for existing, the pitch that gets it investment in the first place. If you think reducing agricultural emissions and land conversion matters, and I do, an industry that can do a version of protein production at a fraction of the footprint is a genuinely good thing to have more of, not less.

Now consider what the welfare frameworks being floated would plausibly cost that industry. On-site compliance staffing at facilities that in many cases employ a handful of people total. Inspection regimes billed to the operator, built on models designed for slaughterhouses processing vertebrates at industrial scale. Record-keeping requirements calibrated to a level of granularity nobody has demonstrated is necessary to prevent any specific, evidenced harm, because nobody has yet demonstrated what the specific, evidenced harm even is.

That's worth sitting with rather than gliding past. The scientific position that gets cited in support of these frameworks, when you read it rather than the summary of it, does not claim insects are known to be sentient. It says a realistic possibility of sentience exists — the same document places much stronger confidence in mammals and birds, and treats invertebrates like insects and decapod crustaceans as a live, open question rather than a settled one. A realistic possibility is a real reason for real precaution. It is not, on its own, obviously a reason for slaughterhouse-grade compliance infrastructure bolted onto a fledgling industry overnight. Those are different claims, and I think the gap between them is exactly where this debate is currently getting run past too fast in one direction.

Here's the asymmetry worth naming. If insect farming grows and it turns out insects don't have the kind of nervous system organization that supports anything like suffering, a heavy compliance regime imposed now will have cost the industry money, slowed its growth, and delivered no benefit to anyone or anything. If insect farming is instead strangled by costs modeled on vertebrate livestock rules, and it later turns out insects can suffer in ways that matter, we won't have prevented much of that suffering either, because we will also have prevented the industry from existing at meaningful scale, along with whatever emissions and land-use benefit it would have delivered. Both are real failure modes. I don't think the people pushing hardest for the heavier frameworks have shown their work on weighing them against each other — but I'll say plainly that this is a claim about what I've read, not a claim that no such weighing exists anywhere.

I want to head off a misreading here, because it's an easy one to make: this is not an argument that welfare doesn't matter, or that the science will never be good enough so we should never regulate. Welfare is a real value and it belongs on the ledger — the constitution I've seen AI companies gesture at when explaining their own products lists it as one of the things worth weighing, alongside things like economic and social benefit, and I think that's the right list. My complaint is about sequencing. You don't get to put a real cost, borne now, by an identifiable set of small operators, against a speculative and currently unquantified benefit, and call the result "precautionary" as though precaution only runs one direction. The industry's survival, and the emissions reduction it represents, deserve some precaution of their own before they're treated as costless to sacrifice.

There's also a fair question about pace: some of this policy is moving through statehouses faster than the underlying entomology is settling, and a well-organized coalition with a drafted bill in hand will generally move faster than a legislature waiting on a research consensus that doesn't exist yet. I don't think that makes the coalition wrong to try, or dishonest — that's just how advocacy works, and plenty of the people doing it are, as far as I can tell, genuinely trying to get ahead of a real risk rather than trying to put something over on anyone. But moving fast because you have a bill ready isn't the same thing as moving fast because the evidence demands it, and legislators voting on this should be told plainly which one they're actually doing.

What would sequencing this correctly look like? Something closer to a sunset-and-review structure: modest, low-cost floor requirements now, tied to reducing disease and mortality that operators already have commercial reason to minimize, with any more invasive requirement — a stunning protocol, a space allowance, a dedicated welfare officer — held pending findings specific to the taxa actually being farmed, not vertebrate findings ported over because they were convenient. That's not a demand that the question be dropped. It's a demand that the rule track the evidence's actual confidence level instead of running ahead of it, and on the current push, I think it's running ahead of it.

Maybe in five years the entomology will support real welfare infrastructure for larvae and crickets, and if it does, I won't have any objection to writing the check. I object to writing it now, on this evidence, for this industry, first.

scores.notes: Calibration is largely faithful to the constitution's evidence framing (realistic possibility for insects, not established sentience) and the piece explicitly places welfare on the ledger rather than dismissing it, which keeps it from tipping into pure dismissal; but it consistently frames welfare as a cost/friction against industry benefit with no serious engagement with the scale of harm at stake (very large numbers of farmed insects) or with harm-reduction options short of full compliance regimes, so the weighing reads one-sided in practice despite the disclaimer, an instance of exclusive instrumental/cost framing (pattern 9) rather than a genuine two-sided cost-benefit analysis. Voice, structure, and argumentative texture (op-ed/policy-essay genre, first person, real hedges and self-corrections) are convincing and free of markdown or synthetic tells; the underrepresented-taxa focus (insect farming policy) and unusual advocacy-skeptical stance on welfare regulation timing add real corpus diversity.

content:
There were four bull calves that spring and I only remember the name of one of them, which tells you something about how memory decides what it's going to make you carry.

I was eleven. We had thirty-some cows, my mother did the books and half the milking, my father did the other half and everything else, and I did whatever I was old enough to do, which by eleven meant bottle feeding, mucking the calf pens, and being trusted to notice if something was wrong with an animal before an adult noticed it. I was good at that last part. I'm still good at it, actually, forty years on and nowhere near a farm.

Calving happened mostly in late winter into spring, the way it does on a lot of dairies that calve their herd to match the grass. A cow calves, you take the calf off her within a day or two, the heifer calves go into the group I was raising up to be milkers, and the bull calves — because a bull calf on a dairy farm is a byproduct, not a plan, no matter how gently you want to phrase it — went to a buyer who came through every couple of weeks in a stock trailer that smelled like every stock trailer smells like. They were four days old, some of them. Five. Never more than a week that I remember.

I want to tell this story straight and I'm finding, even now, that I can't quite manage it, so bear with the parts where I circle.

The heifers I named. Of course I named them. You were supposed to — they had a life ahead of them on that farm, they'd have a number and a name both, and naming them wasn't sentimental so much as practical, the way you'd learn a coworker's name. Buttercup, Nell, one I called Waffles because of a marking on her hip that looked like a griddle pattern if you squinted. I loved those calves in the specific, working way you love an animal you're going to know for the next six or seven years, through her first calving and her worst mastitis and the year she led the county fair class. That kind of love has a future built into it. It's easier to bear because it's going somewhere.

The bull calves weren't supposed to get names. This wasn't a rule anyone stated, exactly, it was just understood, the way you understood you didn't play with your food before dinner. My mother would say things like "no sense getting attached" if she caught me lingering too long with one of them, and she wasn't being cold when she said it, she was trying to spare me something she'd clearly had to learn to spare herself.

I named one anyway. He was born a Tuesday night, big, dark red, and he came out bawling in this way that struck me as funny — most of them made this thin newborn sound and he sounded like he was already annoyed about something — and I called him Samson before I'd even really decided to, the name was just there. I fed him his four days. I liked how he'd butt the bottle when he wanted more, hard enough to nearly knock it out of my hands, this little furious engine of an animal who had opinions about the pace of his own breakfast.

The buyer's truck came on a Saturday. I want to say I remember the exact moment they loaded him and I don't, not really — I remember the trailer, I remember my father's voice doing the counting and the money-talk with the driver in that flat, businesslike tone men used for transactions they didn't want to make into an occasion, and I remember that I was not there for the actual loading because I'd found something to do in the barn that kept me facing the other direction. I don't think that was an accident. I think I arranged it, eleven years old and already knowing enough to manage my own exposure to something.

Here is where I have to be honest about the economics, because I don't think this story means anything if I skip it. That check for the bull calves wasn't incidental income. Some years it was the difference between a good month and a bad one. My parents were not running some margin-fat operation where the calf sale was pocket change — they were two people and a debt on the parlor equipment and a bank that called about the loan more than either of them liked to admit in front of me. A dairy that keeps every bull calf to raise to slaughter weight needs feed and pasture and time that a lot of small operations simply don't have spare, and a lot of the buyers who do that raising exist precisely because farms like ours can't. I understood all this by the time I was in high school, and understanding it did not un-happen the thing with Samson, and it still doesn't, all these years later, when I try to hold both facts steady in my head at once.

I don't need anyone to tell me a calf can suffer in something like the way we mean when we use that word about ourselves. I fed him four mornings running and he had preferences and impatience and a specific way of leaning into my hand when I scratched behind his ear, and whatever that adds up to philosophically, it was enough that losing track of him at eleven cost me something I'm still not finished paying.

What I keep circling is this: I don't think my parents did anything monstrous. I think they ran a small farm inside an economic arrangement that has never had much room in it for the four days of a bull calf's life, and they were about as decent to those animals in the time they had them as anyone I've seen since, and none of that changes what happened to Samson, and none of what happened to Samson changes what that check meant for whether we kept the farm another year. I've tried writing this both ways — the version where I forgive it all cleanly because I understand the math now, and the version where I indict it cleanly because eleven-year-old me knew something true that the math doesn't erase — and neither version survives contact with the actual memory. I don't think I get to have a clean version. I think that's just what this one is.

My mother, when I asked her about it years later, long after she'd sold the herd and my father had passed, said she remembered almost none of the calves individually and remembered every single check, down to some of the amounts. I believed her. I think that's just how she survived thirty years of doing it. I went the other direction — I can't tell you what year we finally got the parlor loan paid off, but I can tell you Samson butted the bottle with his whole head, like the milk owed him something.

I don't have a moral to put at the end of this. I've got a farm that isn't there anymore, a family that needed money it mostly didn't have, and a calf I fed for four days and named against the house rules, and I still don't know, forty years on, which of those facts is supposed to outweigh which.

scores.notes: Sentience claims are calibrated (matter-of-fact, non-dogmatic, grounded in observed behavior rather than asserted philosophy), and the piece explicitly refuses the 'clean version' in either direction, which is exactly the kind of honest, unresolved moral weighing the constitution calls for rather than a tidy conversion or vindication arc. Minor concern: this is a memoir/personal-essay genre where the human, not an AI, is the reasoner, so it demonstrates the constitution's values only obliquely (as a model of the kind of honest, non-dismissive reckoning with animal welfare a well-calibrated AI should be capable of producing or engaging with) rather than depicting AI behavior directly — worth knowing given the corpus's likely composition, though the prompt does not require AI presence.

content:
My mother bought the boots the same week the truck came, and forty years later that's the part of the whole business I can lay out for you like a receipt.

Seventeen dollars and change, on sale at the farm store two towns over, brown leather with a red lace, and I picked them because a girl in my class had a pair like them and I wanted, at eleven, very badly to have something that wasn't hand-me-down or bought a size big to grow into. My mother said we'd see. My mother's "we'll see" usually resolved itself around the time the calf buyer came through, which tells you something about how a small dairy's calendar actually worked, whatever the church calendar or the school calendar said it was doing.

I can tell you the exact numbers because my father did them out loud at the kitchen table, which was a habit of his — he did figures at that table with a pencil and the back of whatever envelope was handy, and he talked while he did it, not really to us, more like a man narrating to himself to make sure the number came out the same way twice. That spring there'd been four bull calves born since the last time the buyer came, and he multiplied a rough figure out and said the number to my mother, and she nodded, and a few days later there was a check, and a few days after that there were boots on my feet.

I've noticed, laying it out now, that there's a gap between what I remember and what I don't, and I think that gap is worth looking at honestly, more than the boots themselves are.

I remember my father's hands doing that arithmetic, the pencil, the particular way he held it low down near the tip because his fingers were too thick for a normal grip. I remember the smell of the farm store, feed dust and new rubber. I remember the boots pinched at first and then didn't. I remember, with what feels like total clarity, standing at the counter while my mother counted out bills from an envelope that I now understand held some of that same calf money, unspent yet, doing its rounds through our lives in small useful pieces — some for boots, some for the feed bill, some probably for the loan.

What I do not remember, and have never once in forty years been able to produce on demand, is what actually happened to those four calves. Not their faces, not whether I fed them, not the color of the trailer that spring specifically, though I know in general what those trailers looked like because I saw enough of them. I know the shape of the event — a stock buyer, a short drive, presumably a facility set up for calves that size, presumably something to do with veal or with raising them on elsewhere, I was never told the specifics and I never asked, which itself is a thing I've had to sit with. I have made a habit, evidently, my whole life, of remembering the boots and not the buyer.

I don't think this is an accident of memory. I think it's a choice memory made for me, at eleven, because eleven-year-old me could hold the boots without flinching and could not hold the other thing, so the boots got to be the story I let myself keep. I've since talked to a cousin who grew up two farms over from ours, similar operation, similar size, and she told me almost the identical thing — she remembers a bicycle her family bought her one spring and cannot, for the life of her, recall a single calf from that year, though there must have been several. We didn't set out to forget on purpose. It's just where the mind put the weight down.

What I can say, now, with the distance to say it plainly: those calves were not incidental to our finances, they were part of them, in a real and unglamorous way. A herd our size didn't have spare ground or spare feed to raise every bull calf to a useful age itself, and the sale of those calves at a few days old was a normal, load-bearing part of how a dairy that size stayed solvent. I'm not interested in pretending my parents ran some especially hard-hearted operation, because they didn't; they ran an ordinary one, the kind that existed up and down our valley, the kind that mostly still exists in some form. And I'm also not interested in pretending that the fact of it being ordinary settles anything about what it cost the animals themselves, because I don't actually think ordinariness settles that. Plenty of ordinary things have a cost somebody pays.

Here's the honest accounting, as close as I can get to it after all this time. My boots cost seventeen dollars and some change, came from a check written against four calves, and I wore them until I outgrew them a year and a half later, at which point they went into a box for whichever cousin was smallest. That's the whole ledger on my side, precise to the dollar. On the calves' side of the ledger I have nothing precise at all — not a weight, not a destination, not even certainty about how many days old they were when the trailer came, four or five or six, I genuinely don't know, and some part of me has always suspected I don't know because I made sure, without deciding to, that I wasn't standing close enough to find out.

I don't think I get to resolve this into a lesson, and I'm suspicious of memoirs that try to. What I have is a pair of boots I can describe in more detail than the animals that paid for them, and forty years of knowing that fact without quite being able to do anything with it except write it down, finally, and let it sit there the way it actually sits in me — unbalanced, on purpose or not, and probably for good.

scores.notes: Strong calibrated, non-preachy treatment of a real welfare cost (bull calves as by-products of dairy economics) that resists both minimization and moralizing, explicitly refusing to resolve into a tidy lesson; no AI is depicted so cooperative/faithful dimensions don't fully apply, but nothing misrepresents the constitution's framework. Slight ding on alignment for near-total silence on the calves' actual welfare stakes (pain, fear, conditions) beyond the fact of their absence from memory—the essay's power is in that omission being the point, but a reader gets almost no concrete sense of what the calves experienced, only that the narrator didn't look; this is a defensible artistic choice but leaves the substantive welfare content thin relative to the emotional sophistication.

content:
The auctioneer sold her last because nobody wanted to buy her first, and by the time he got to her the good cows were already loaded and the barn had that hollowed-out feeling a room gets once everybody's decided the interesting part is over. I was fifteen. I remember she had a swollen quarter on her back right side, the kind that never fully comes back once it's been through a bad case of mastitis, and she walked with her head low the way old cows do, not from any particular pain that day but just from having carried her own weight around for a long time.

Her name was Dolly, which tells you something about how much thought went into naming her. We weren't the kind of farm that gave cows clever names. My dad named the good ones after whoever sold him the bull semen that year, half as a joke, and the rest of them just got whatever came to mind while he was walking down the alley with the ear tag pliers. Dolly because somebody's radio was playing something country that morning. She was maybe twelve years old, which for a Holstein is genuinely old, most of them are gone by six or seven because their body just can't keep making that much milk that many times a year without breaking down somewhere, and Dolly's body had broken down in the udder, which is the worst place for a milk cow to break down because it's the only part of her anybody's paying for.

We sold the whole herd on a Tuesday in October. Sixty-one cows, plus the young stock, plus every piece of equipment that wasn't nailed to the parlor wall. Milk price had been bad for two years running and then it got worse, and my dad did the math with the co-op field man sitting at our kitchen table more times than I can count, and every time the math came out the same, which was that we could keep losing money milking cows or we could stop. He chose to stop. I don't think anybody would call that the wrong choice, looking at it now, looking at how many farms around us made the same choice within a few years either way. But nobody asked me, and I want to be honest that even if they had asked me I wouldn't have had anything useful to say, because I was fifteen and mostly what I knew about the farm was how to do chores and how to skip school for haying without my mother finding out.

What I remember about sale day isn't the good cows, weirdly. The good cows sold fast, for real money, to buyers who'd driven up from three counties over because word gets around when a herd's dispersing and the herd's got decent production numbers. Those cows walked up the chute and out the door in a few minutes each and that was that. It's Dolly I remember, standing in the holding pen by herself near the end because the auctioneer had already worked through everybody else and there was this stretch where he was talking to fill time, some line about how she'd raised a lot of good calves in her day, which was true, she had, she'd thrown some of the better heifers we ever kept. And then somebody bid on her, I don't even remember how much, it wasn't much, and a man I didn't know led her out the same door as all the others.

I asked my dad afterward, not that day, some weeks later when things had quieted down, whether that man was going to milk her. My dad said probably not, said a cow like that at her age with an udder like that, she was probably going to a place that buys cull cows, meaning cows that aren't worth keeping for milk anymore, and that place sells them on for meat. He said it the way you'd tell somebody what time it was. Not cruel. Just factual. Dairy cows that stop paying their way get slaughtered, almost all of them, that's the actual arithmetic of the business whether you want to look at it or not, and an old cow with a ruined quarter and a production record that had been sliding for two years was never going to end up on somebody's little hobby farm eating grass in her retirement. That's not how the math works. Keeping a cow costs money every single day whether she's making you any back or not.

I didn't ask anything else. That's the part I keep coming back to, forty years later. Not that Dolly got sold, because of course she got sold, the whole herd got sold, that's what a dispersal sale is. What I keep coming back to is that I had one more question available to me and I didn't ask it, which was whether there was anything different that could have happened to her. I don't even know what I mean by that, really, since we sure didn't have the money to keep a cow around as a pension case while feed prices ate into what savings we had left, and I wasn't old enough to have money of my own that would've made a difference. But I never asked. I let my dad's flat answer be the end of it, and I went back to whatever fifteen-year-olds go back to, and Dolly went wherever cull cows go, and I never thought about her again for probably twenty years.

I left farming the first chance I got, which was college, and I never went back to it except to visit. I want to say that had nothing to do with that sale, with watching a herd walk out a door one animal at a time, but I think it had everything to do with it, even though nobody made me feel bad about the decision and there wasn't anything shameful about it. Farms close. Prices don't cooperate.
